description: Prophets from Adam ﷺ to Isa ﷺ — stories, missions, miracles

questions.q: What did Iblis destroy first in his attempt to make Ayyub disbelieve?
questions.explanation: Iblis first targeted Ayyub's wealth to test his faith, hoping that losing material possessions would lead him to despair and disbelief. Despite this, Ayyub remained patient and steadfast in his trust in Allah.

questions.q: How did Ayyub respond when his children were killed?
opts: B. He remained patient and thankful to Allah
C. He became angry with Allah
questions.explanation: Prophet Ayyub ﷺ showed immense patience and gratitude to Allah despite losing his children, demonstrating his unwavering faith and trust in God's wisdom during severe trials. This teaches the importance of patience and thankfulness in times of hardship.

questions.q: What did Ayyub's wife suggest he do after years of suffering?
opts: B. Call upon Allah to remove the suffering
questions.explanation: Ayyub's wife encouraged him to be patient and to call upon Allah for relief, emphasizing reliance on God's mercy during hardship. This reflects the Islamic teaching of turning to Allah in prayer and patience when facing trials.

questions.q: Who brought the throne of Bilqis to Sulaiman in the twinkling of an eye?
opts: B. One who had knowledge of the Scripture
questions.explanation: A person with knowledge of the Scripture, often understood as a powerful and knowledgeable jinn or angel, brought the throne of Bilqis to Prophet Sulaiman ﷺ instantly, demonstrating Allah’s power and Sulaiman’s unique authority granted by divine wisdom.

questions.q: According to Ibn Kathir's description, what is the relationship between the Throne (`Arsh) and the Kursi (Footstool)?
opts: A. They are the same thing with two different names
B. The Kursi is above the Throne and contains it
C. The Throne is above the Kursi and is greater in size than it
D. The Kursi is inside the Throne and carries it
questions.explanation: Ibn Kathir explains that the Throne (`Arsh) is positioned above the Kursi (Footstool) and is much larger in size, symbolizing the greatness and majesty of Allah's dominion over the heavens and the earth. This hierarchy emphasizes Allah’s supreme authority.

questions.q: According to the authentic narrations compiled by Ibn Kathir, what was the very first thing Allah created?
questions.explanation: According to authentic narrations cited by Ibn Kathir, Allah first created the Pen (Qalam), which was commanded to write everything that will happen until the Day of Judgment. This emphasizes Allah’s knowledge and decree over all creation from the very beginning.

questions.q: What was the very first thing Allah created, according to authentic narrations mentioned in Volume 1?
questions.explanation: According to authentic narrations, the very first thing Allah created was the Pen (Al-Qalam), which He commanded to write everything that would happen until the Day of Judgment. This highlights the importance of knowledge and divine decree in Islam.

questions.q: After creating the Pen, what command did Allah give it?
opts: B. To write the destinies of all creation until the Day of Judgment
C. To measure the heavens and earth
D. To record the names of the prophets
questions.explanation: After creating the Pen, Allah commanded it to write everything that will happen, including the destinies of all creation until the Day of Judgment. This highlights Allah's knowledge and control over all events in the universe.

questions.q: When were the Jinn created in relation to humans?
opts: C. At the same time as Adam
D. After the angels but before Adam
questions.explanation: Jinn were created from smokeless fire before the creation of Adam, who was made from clay. This is mentioned in the Quran, highlighting that jinn existed prior to humans.

questions.q: When Allah created the Pen, what command did He give it?
opts: B. To write the destinies of all creation until the Day of Judgment
C. To measure the distances between the heavens
D. To record the names of the prophets
questions.explanation: When Allah created the Pen, He commanded it to write everything that would happen until the Day of Judgment, including the destinies of all creation. This shows Allah’s knowledge and control over all events in the universe.

questions.q: According to a different authentic narration, what existed before the Throne?
questions.explanation: Before the Throne (Arsh) of Allah, it is authentically narrated that water existed, symbolizing the primordial creation from which everything else was made. This highlights the concept of divine creation beginning from a fundamental, life-giving element.

questions.q: How many angels carry the Throne of Allah on the Day of Judgment, as mentioned in the Quran and explained by Ibn Kathir?
questions.explanation: The Quran mentions in Surah Al-Haqqa (69:17) that eight angels will carry the Throne of Allah on the Day of Judgment. Ibn Kathir explains this to emphasize the immense power and majesty of Allah’s creation during that significant event.

questions.q: Where was the Throne of Allah before the creation of the heavens and earth?
questions.explanation: Before creating the heavens and the earth, Allah's Throne was established over the water, as mentioned in the Qur'an (Surah Hud 11:7). This signifies Allah's supreme authority and control over all creation from the very beginning.

questions.q: According to the hadith of Abu Dharr, what fills every span of four fingers in the heavens?
questions.explanation: In the hadith of Abu Dharr, the Prophet ﷺ describes that every span of four fingers in the heavens is filled with angels prostrating to Allah, illustrating their continuous worship and submission. This emphasizes the vastness of the heavens and the constant glorification of Allah by His angels.

questions.q: What is the wisdom behind Allah creating the universe in six days when He could have created it in an instant with the word "Be"?
opts: A. To teach angels the process of creation
B. To demonstrate His power gradually
C. To teach mankind patience and to show that matters unfold gradually with wisdom
D. Because the materials needed time to form
questions.explanation: Creating the universe in six days demonstrates Allah’s wisdom in gradual development, teaching humans patience and that important matters require time to unfold properly, rather than happening instantly without purpose or order.

questions.q: In the hadith about the creation of the Pen, what did the Pen write first?
opts: A. The names of the prophets
C. The destinies of all creation until the Hour
D. The creation of the Throne
questions.explanation: The Pen first wrote the destinies of all creation until the Hour, indicating that everything is preordained by Allah's knowledge and decree. This highlights the concept of Qadar (divine predestination) in Islam, showing that all events are known and recorded before they occur.

questions.q: What was the span of Jibril's wings according to the hadith of Ibn Mas'ud?
opts: A. Each wing covered the distance between east and west
B. Each wing covered the entire earth
C. Each wing reached from the earth to the Throne
D. Each wing was the size of the seven heavens
questions.explanation: According to the hadith narrated by Ibn Mas'ud, the angel Jibril's wings were so vast that each wing stretched from the east to the west, illustrating his immense size and power as a messenger of Allah. This emphasizes the grandeur and majesty of angels in Islamic teachings.

questions.q: According to Ibn Kathir's methodology, what is his position on narrations that discuss what was created first when there are multiple authentic narrations?
opts: A. He rejects all of them
B. He accepts only one as correct and rejects others
C. He acknowledges that such matters are from the unseen and we accept all authentic narrations without attempting to reconcile impossible contradictions
D. He prefers Isra'iliyyat over Islamic narrations
questions.explanation: Ibn Kathir recognizes that questions about what was created first relate to the unseen (ghayb), so when multiple authentic narrations differ, he accepts them all without forcing a reconciliation, understanding that human knowledge is limited in such matters.

questions.q: In the hadith where the Prophet ﷺ asks his companions about what is above them, what answer does he give when they say "Allah and His Messenger know best"?
opts: B. "It is the roof of Paradise"
C. "It is Al-Rafi` (the canopy), a protected ceiling and a withheld wave"
D. "It is the highest heaven where Adam resides"
questions.explanation: When the companions respond with "Allah and His Messenger know best," the Prophet ﷺ explains that above them is Al-Rafi`—a protected ceiling and a withheld wave—indicating a divine barrier that safeguards the heavens and earth, emphasizing Allah’s control over the unseen.

questions.q: What is the relationship between the Throne (`Arsh) and the Kursi (Footstool)?
opts: A. They are the same thing
B. The Kursi is inside the Throne and smaller
C. The Throne is above the Kursi and encompasses it, just as the Kursi encompasses the heavens
D. The Kursi is above the Throne
questions.explanation: The Throne (`Arsh) is greater and positioned above the Kursi (Footstool), symbolizing Allah’s supreme authority. The Kursi encompasses the heavens, while the Throne encompasses the Kursi, illustrating the vastness of Allah’s dominion and power.

questions.q: What did Ibn Abbas say about the Kursi in relation to the heavens?
opts: A. The Kursi is the seventh heaven
B. The Kursi is the place of the feet, and the Throne is above it, and the heavens compared to the Kursi are like a ring in a desert
C. The Kursi is smaller than the first heaven
D. The Kursi is the Throne itself
questions.explanation: Ibn Abbas explained that the Kursi (Footstool) is beneath the Throne, indicating a hierarchy in the unseen realm, and illustrated the vastness of the heavens by comparing them to a small ring in a desert relative to the Kursi, emphasizing Allah’s immense creation beyond human comprehension.

questions.q: What does the Verse of the Kursi (Ayat al-Kursi) describe about Allah's sovereignty?
opts: A. That His Throne extends over the heavens and the earth
B. That He sits on the Throne
C. That the Throne is limited to the seventh heaven
D. That the Kursi is His dwelling place
questions.explanation: Ayat al-Kursi highlights Allah's immense sovereignty by stating that His Throne extends over the heavens and the earth, symbolizing His supreme authority and control over all creation. This verse emphasizes His power, knowledge, and guardianship over everything in existence.

questions.q: What happens to disbelieving Jinn on the Day of Judgment?
opts: A. They enter Hellfire with Iblis
B. They are forgiven because they were not sent prophets
questions.explanation: Disbelieving Jinn, like Iblis, rejected Allah's guidance and will be punished on the Day of Judgment by entering Hellfire as a consequence of their disbelief and disobedience. This is mentioned in the Qur'an, highlighting their accountability similar to that of disbelieving humans.

questions.q: What did Allah create the heavens from, according to Surah Fussilat?
questions.explanation: Surah Fussilat (41:11) mentions that Allah created the heavens from "smoke" (dukhan), indicating a primordial, gaseous state before the formation of the cosmos. This reflects the Quran's description of the universe's early creation stages in a way that aligns with the concept of a formless, misty origin.

questions.q: Was Iblis an angel or a jinn?
opts: D. He was a unique creation
questions.explanation: Iblis was a jinn, not an angel, because angels are created from light and always obey Allah, while jinn are created from smokeless fire and have free will, allowing Iblis to disobey Allah's command to bow to Adam ﷺ.

questions.q: If Iblis was a jinn, how did he come to be among the angels in heaven?
opts: B. He worshipped with the angels due to his devotion and was elevated to their company
C. He captured an angel and took his place
D. He was an angel who turned into a jinn
questions.explanation: Iblis was a jinn created from smokeless fire, but because of his sincere worship and devotion, he was allowed to be among the angels in heaven. His elevated status was due to his piety, not his nature, which later led to his refusal to bow to Adam ﷺ.

questions.q: How long did Iblis worship Allah with the angels?
opts: C. A period known only to Allah - narrations vary and are from Isra'iliyyat
questions.explanation: The exact duration of Iblis worshipping Allah with the angels is not specified in authentic Islamic texts, and narrations about this vary, often coming from Isra'iliyyat (Jewish or Christian traditions). Only Allah knows the true length of this period.

questions.q: What was Iblis's rank among the angels?
opts: A. He was the leader of the angels
B. He was a teacher of the angels
C. He was a renowned worshipper and scholar among them
D. He was the lowest among them
questions.explanation: Iblis was not actually an angel but a jinn who was highly knowledgeable and devoted in worship, which earned him a respected status among the angels before his refusal to bow to Adam ﷺ. This distinction highlights his unique position and subsequent fall from grace.

questions.q: What does Surah al-Kahf (18:50) explicitly state about Iblis's origin?
opts: B. "He was from the jinn, so he disobeyed the command of his Lord"
C. "He was from the humans who were elevated"
questions.explanation: Surah al-Kahf (18:50) clarifies that Iblis was created from the jinn, not from angels, which explains why he had free will and chose to disobey Allah's command to prostrate to Adam ﷺ. This distinction highlights the nature of Iblis's rebellion.

questions.q: What was the test that led to Iblis's fall?
opts: A. He was asked to fast for a thousand years
B. He was commanded to prostrate to Adam
C. He was asked to surrender his position
D. He was tested with wealth
questions.explanation: Iblis was commanded by Allah to prostrate to Adam as a sign of respect and obedience, but he refused out of arrogance, claiming he was superior. This disobedience led to his fall and expulsion from Allah’s mercy.

questions.q: What logical fallacy did Iblis commit in his argument?
opts: A. He compared two different things
B. He judged superiority by substance rather than by obedience to Allah's command
C. He forgot that Allah's command supersedes all reasoning
questions.explanation: Iblis committed multiple logical fallacies, including arrogance (assuming superiority over Adam ﷺ), false cause (believing his creation from fire made him inherently better than Adam from clay), and ignoring God's command. These errors led to his refusal to obey Allah's order to prostrate to Adam ﷺ.

questions.q: What was Allah's response to Iblis's refusal?
opts: A. "Then get out of it (Paradise/heaven), for indeed you are accursed"
questions.explanation: When Iblis refused to obey Allah's command to bow to Adam ﷺ, Allah expelled him from Paradise and cursed him because of his disobedience and arrogance, emphasizing the consequences of defying divine commands.

questions.q: What did Iblis ask Allah after being cursed?
opts: B. To be given respite until the Day of Judgment
C. To be given another chance to prostrate
questions.explanation: Iblis asked Allah to grant him respite until the Day of Judgment so he could mislead humans and prove that they are unworthy of Allah’s favor. This request was granted, allowing Iblis to tempt people until the end of time.

questions.q: Did Allah grant Iblis's request for respite?
opts: B. Yes, until the Day of Judgment
C. Yes, until the appointed time (the Day of Judgment or the first blast)
D. Yes, for one thousand years
questions.explanation: Allah granted Iblis's request for respite until the Day of Judgment or the first blast, allowing him to mislead humans temporarily. This delay serves as a test for human free will and faith.

questions.q: Why did Allah grant Iblis respite despite his disobedience?
opts: B. As a test for humanity and to fulfill divine wisdom
C. Because Iblis begged for it
D. Because the angels interceded for him
questions.explanation: Allah granted Iblis respite to test human beings by allowing the presence of evil and temptation, which helps distinguish true faith and obedience. This delay also serves to fulfill divine wisdom and the greater plan of guidance and accountability in life.

questions.q: According to the hadith of Abu Dharr, what fills the space of four fingers in the heavens?
opts: C. The light of the Throne
questions.explanation: In the hadith of Abu Dharr, it is mentioned that the space of four fingers in the heavens is filled with angels prostrating to Allah, showing their continuous worship and submission. This highlights the vast number of angels and their constant devotion in the unseen realms.

questions.q: What did Iblis swear to do to the children of Adam?
opts: B. To mislead them all except the sincere servants of Allah
D. To make them forget Allah
questions.explanation: Iblis vowed to misguide the descendants of Adam by tempting them away from Allah's path, except for those who are truly devoted and sincere servants of Allah, who remain protected by His guidance and mercy.

questions.q: According to Surah al-Isra (17:64-65), what resources did Allah permit Iblis to use?
opts: A. His voice, his cavalry and infantry, sharing in wealth and children, and making promises
questions.explanation: Allah allowed Iblis to use his voice to whisper and deceive, along with cavalry and infantry symbolizing forces of evil, to tempt humans through wealth, children, and false promises, as mentioned in Surah al-Isra (17:64-65), illustrating the means by which Satan tries to mislead people.

questions.q: What does "sharing in wealth and children" mean according to the commentators?
opts: A. Iblis owns half of all human wealth
B. Every wealth that is not purified by Zakah, and every child born out of wedlock or not named properly
C. Iblis can take people's children
questions.explanation: "Sharing in wealth and children" refers to possessing wealth without fulfilling the obligation of Zakah purification, and having children either born outside of lawful marriage or not given proper Islamic names, which affects their social and religious identity according to commentators.

questions.q: Does Iblis have offspring?
opts: B. Yes, he has children and descendants who help him
D. He creates demons from fire
questions.explanation: Iblis is believed to have offspring who assist him in leading humans astray, as mentioned in Islamic teachings. These descendants work to spread evil and tempt people away from the path of Allah.

questions.q: What are the names of some of Iblis's offspring mentioned in some narrations?
opts: C. Awar, Zalambur, etc. - but these are from Isra'iliyyat
D. They are not named in authentic sources
questions.explanation: Some narrations mention Iblis's offspring like Awar and Zalambur, but these names come from Isra'iliyyat—stories of Jewish or Christian origin incorporated into Islamic tradition. Such details are not confirmed by authentic Islamic texts and should be understood cautiously.

questions.q: According to Ibn Kathir's commentary, why did the angels appear in this form to Lut?
opts: A. Because they could not appear in their true form
B. As a test for Lut and his people
C. Because Lut requested to see them that way
D. To hide from Iblis who was following them
questions.explanation: The angels appeared in human form to Lut as a test to see how he and his people would respond to strangers and guests, highlighting their hospitality and morality, which was a common way prophets were tested in Islamic tradition.

questions.q: How does Ibn Kathir reconcile the verse that says the earth was created in two days (Surah Fussilat) with the hadith that spreads creation over specific days?
opts: B. He explains that the "two days" refer to the Sunday and Monday mentioned in the hadith, and the "four days" for provisions include Tuesday through Friday
C. He says the hadith is weak and cannot be used
D. He says the Quranic days are not the same as earthly days
questions.explanation: Ibn Kathir clarifies that the "two days" in Surah Fussilat correspond to Sunday and Monday, during which the earth was created, while the "four days" for preparing provisions refer to Tuesday through Friday, aligning the Quranic verse with the hadith's detailed timeline of creation.

questions.q: How did Iblis enter Paradise to deceive Adam and Hawwa after being expelled?
opts: A. He walked through the gates
B. He hid in the mouth of the snake
C. He flew over the walls
D. He deceived the guardian angels
questions.explanation: Iblis entered Paradise by hiding in the mouth of the snake, which allowed him to approach Adam and Hawwa without being noticed and deceive them into eating from the forbidden tree. This method is mentioned in Islamic tradition to explain how he bypassed the angels' vigilance.

questions.q: What did Iblis whisper to Adam and Hawwa?
opts: A. "Eat from this tree and you will become angels"
B. "Eat from this tree and you will live forever (khuld)"
C. "Eat from this tree and you will have eternal kingdom"
questions.explanation: Iblis whispered to Adam and Hawwa to eat from the forbidden tree, promising them that it would grant them immortality and a kingdom that never decays, tempting them to disobey Allah's command. This led to their slip, highlighting the danger of succumbing to Satan's deceit.

questions.q: How did idol worship begin among the descendants of Adam?
opts: A. It was revealed by Allah
B. Some people made images of righteous people who had died, to remember them and be inspired, then later generations worshipped these images
questions.explanation: Idol worship began when some descendants of Adam created images of righteous ancestors to honor and remember them, but over time, later generations started worshipping these images themselves instead of God, leading to the practice of idol worship.

questions.q: What linguistic point does Ibn Kathir make about the conjunction "waw" (and) in verses about creation?
opts: A. It always implies chronological sequence
B. It does not necessarily imply order; it is used for absolute conjunction
C. It proves the earth was created before the heavens
D. It is a grammatical error
questions.explanation: Ibn Kathir explains that the conjunction "waw" (and) in creation verses does not always indicate a chronological sequence but serves as an absolute conjunction, linking concepts without implying order. This highlights the flexibility of Arabic grammar in conveying meanings beyond strict temporal progression.

questions.q: What oath did Iblis swear to convince Adam and Hawwa?
opts: A. "I swear by Allah that I am your advisor"
B. "Indeed, I am to you from among the sincere advisors"
C. "I swear I am telling the truth"
D. "By Allah, this tree is blessed"
questions.explanation: Iblis swore the oath "Indeed, I am to you from among the sincere advisors" to deceive Adam and Hawwa by pretending to offer genuine guidance, while his true intention was to mislead them away from Allah's command. This highlights his role as a deceiver in Islamic teachings.

questions.q: What was the name of the person who first suggested making images of the righteous?
opts: B. A man named Yarub or something similar, according to some narrations
questions.explanation: A man named Yarub is mentioned in some narrations as the first to suggest making images of the righteous, aiming to honor and remember them. This practice reflects early human attempts to preserve the memory of virtuous individuals visually.

questions.q: Who ate from the tree first, according to many narrations?
questions.explanation: Many narrations state that Hawwa (Eve) ate from the forbidden tree first, which led to both her and Adam ﷺ being tested and subsequently repenting. This highlights the human aspect of their story and the importance of seeking forgiveness from Allah.

questions.q: How did Iblis exploit this practice?
opts: B. He encouraged people to worship the images after the generation that made them passed away
D. He told people the images were gods
questions.explanation: Iblis exploited the practice of making images by encouraging people to worship these images even after their creators had died, turning a permissible act into idolatry, which is strictly forbidden in Islam. This led to shirk, associating partners with Allah.

questions.q: From what substance were the Jinn created, according to the Quran and hadith cited by Ibn Kathir?
questions.explanation: The Quran and hadith describe that Jinn were created from "smokeless fire" (Marj min nar), highlighting their spiritual and unseen nature, distinct from humans who were created from clay. This emphasizes the different origins and qualities of these beings in Islamic teachings.

questions.q: What was Iblis's original name before his disobedience?
opts: D. There is no authentic record of his original name
questions.explanation: Before his disobedience, Iblis was known as Azazil, a respected jinn who was elevated among the angels due to his piety. His refusal to bow to Adam ﷺ led to his fall and transformation into Iblis, the accursed.

questions.q: According to one narration cited by Ibn Kathir from Shahr ibn Hawshab, what was the original situation of the Jinn on earth, and what happened to them?
opts: A. They were obedient until Iblis corrupted them
B. They caused corruption on earth, so Allah sent angels who fought them and drove them to the islands of the seas
C. They were created after humans and lived peacefully
D. They were all destroyed except Iblis who repented
questions.explanation: The narration explains that initially, the Jinn lived on earth but caused widespread corruption. To restore order, Allah sent angels who fought the Jinn and forced them to retreat to the islands in the seas, separating them from human habitation.

questions.q: According to the same narration, how did Iblis end up in heaven with the angels?
opts: A. He was an angel originally
B. He was captured as a prisoner by the angels and taken with them to heaven
C. He ascended on his own power
D. He was created in heaven
questions.explanation: Iblis ended up in heaven because the angels captured him as a prisoner during a conflict and brought him along with them. This shows that his presence there was not by choice but by force.

questions.q: What statement of Hasan al-Basri does Ibn Kathir quote about Iblis and the angels?
opts: A. "Iblis was the leader of the angels in the first heaven"
B. "Iblis never had the power to even blink his eyes among the angels"
C. "Iblis taught the angels about worship"
D. "Iblis was created from the same light as the angels but chose fire"
questions.explanation: Hasan al-Basri's statement highlights that Iblis was never truly equal to the angels in status or power; he was always inferior and powerless among them, emphasizing his fall was due to arrogance, not inherent strength. This underscores Iblis's role as a jinn, not an angel.

questions.q: What was Iblis's explicit reason for refusing to prostrate to Adam?
opts: A. He considered Adam less intelligent
B. He claimed he was created from fire while Adam was from clay, and fire is superior
C. He thought prostration should be for Allah alone (pretending piety)
D. He was jealous that Adam was created after him
questions.explanation: Iblis refused to prostrate to Adam because he believed his creation from fire was superior to Adam's creation from clay, showing arrogance and disobedience to Allah's command. This highlights the importance of humility and obedience in Islam, regardless of one's origin.

questions.q: When Iblis asked Allah to be given respite until the Day of Judgment, what was Allah's response?
opts: B. He granted him respite until the Day of Judgment
C. He granted him respite until the appointed time (the Day of Judgment or the first blast)
D. He told him to ask again on the Day of Judgment
questions.explanation: Allah granted Iblis respite until the Day of Judgment, allowing him to mislead humans until that time. This shows Allah's wisdom in testing human free will while setting a clear limit on Iblis's influence.

questions.q: What did Iblis swear to do to the children of Adam after being granted respite?
opts: B. To beautify evil for them on earth and mislead them all except the sincere servants of Allah
C. To make them forget Allah completely
questions.explanation: Iblis vowed to deceive the children of Adam by making evil appear attractive and leading them astray on earth, except for those who are truly devoted and sincere servants of Allah. This highlights the ongoing spiritual struggle between good and evil in human life.

questions.q: According to Surah al-Isra' (17:64-65), what resources did Allah permit Iblis to use in his attempts to mislead humanity?
opts: A. His voice, his cavalry and infantry, sharing in wealth and children, and making promises
questions.explanation: Allah allowed Iblis to use his voice, cavalry, infantry, wealth, children, and promises to try to mislead humans, as mentioned in Surah al-Isra (17:64-65), but He forbade him from harming the sincere servants of Allah, demonstrating both Iblis's limited power and Allah's ultimate control.

questions.q: What does the verse "Share with them in wealth and children" mean according to the commentators cited by Ibn Kathir?
opts: A. Iblis has the power to take people's money and children
B. Every wealth that is not purified by Zakah, and every child born out of wedlock or not named properly
C. Iblis owns half of all human wealth
D. Iblis can possess children at birth
questions.explanation: The verse means that wealth not purified by paying Zakah is shared with others unjustly, and children born outside lawful marriage or without proper naming are not rightfully part of one's inheritance or blessings, as explained by commentators cited by Ibn Kathir.

questions.q: What is the "cavalry and infantry" of Iblis, as explained by the commentators?
opts: A. Actual horses and soldiers from the jinn
B. All those who obey him and spread evil, whether riding or walking
C. The demons who ride on animals and those who walk on the ground
D. The thoughts that come to humans when they are mounted or walking
questions.explanation: The "cavalry and infantry" of Iblis refers metaphorically to all his followers who carry out his commands and spread evil, regardless of their mode of movement, symbolizing the widespread and varied nature of his influence among people.

questions.q: What protection did Allah give to His sincere servants against Iblis?
opts: A. They are completely immune to his whispers
B. He has no authority over them
D. They cannot see Iblis, so he cannot approach them
questions.explanation: Allah grants His sincere servants protection by ensuring that Iblis has no authority or control over them, meaning he cannot force them to sin or harm them spiritually if they remain devoted and seek Allah’s help.

questions.q: After Adam demonstrated his knowledge of the names, what did Allah command the angels to do?
opts: D. To ask Adam for forgiveness
questions.explanation: After Adam ﷺ demonstrated his knowledge of the names, Allah commanded the angels to prostrate to him as a sign of respect and acknowledgment of his special status and knowledge, highlighting the honor given to humans above angels in this context.

questions.q: Who did not prostrate to Adam, and for what reason?
opts: A. Jibril, because he was higher in rank
B. Mika'il, because he was busy with rain
C. Iblis, out of arrogance and jealousy
D. Israfil, because he was preparing to blow the Trumpet
questions.explanation: Iblis refused to prostrate to Adam because of his arrogance and jealousy, believing he was superior as he was created from fire while Adam was made from clay. This act of disobedience led to his expulsion from Paradise.

questions.q: In which seven locations in the Quran does Allah mention the story of Iblis's refusal to prostrate to Adam?
opts: B. Al-Baqarah, Al-A'raf, Al-Hijr, Al-Isra', Al-Kahf, Ta-Ha, and Saad
D. In the Torah and Quran only
questions.explanation: The story of Iblis refusing to prostrate to Adam is mentioned in these seven Quranic chapters to emphasize the significance of obedience to Allah and the consequences of arrogance and disobedience, highlighting key lessons about humility and submission to God's commands.

questions.q: How did Iblis deceive Adam and Hawwa into eating from the forbidden tree?
opts: A. He pushed them towards it
B. He whispered to them, calling it the "Tree of Immortality" and promising that they would become angels or live forever
C. He made it look beautiful and irresistible
D. He told them Allah had changed His command
questions.explanation: Iblis deceived Adam and Hawwa by whispering false promises that eating from the forbidden tree would grant them immortality and angelic status, which tempted them to disobey Allah’s command. This highlights the danger of Satan’s deceit through appealing to human desires.

questions.q: Why did Iblis refuse to prostrate before Adam?
opts: A. He considered himself better because he was created from fire while Adam was from clay
B. He did not hear Allah's command
C. He was waiting for other angels to prostrate first
D. He was not present when the command was given
questions.explanation: Iblis refused to prostrate before Adam because he believed his creation from fire made him superior to Adam, who was made from clay. This arrogance led to his disobedience and subsequent expulsion from Allah’s mercy.

questions.q: From what was Eve (Hawa) created?
opts: A. From the dust of the earth
B. From Adam's shortest left rib while he was sleeping
C. From fire like the jinn
questions.explanation: Eve (Hawa) was created from Adam's shortest left rib while he was sleeping to symbolize their close relationship and equality, emphasizing that she was made from him and not from an entirely different creation. This highlights the unity and companionship between man and woman in Islamic teachings.

questions.q: What did the angels say about Nuh before Iblis became jealous of him?
opts: A. That he was the richest man
B. That he was a man of noble character with great patience who always remembered Allah
C. That he was the strongest warrior
D. That he was the most handsome man
questions.explanation: The angels praised Nuh ﷺ for his noble character and remarkable patience, highlighting his constant remembrance of Allah. This recognition of his virtues led to Iblis's jealousy, as Nuh's devotion and righteousness were highly esteemed by the angels.

questions.q: What did Iblis do when Allah allowed him to test Nuh?
opts: A. He tempted Nuh with wealth
B. He destroyed Nuh's wealth, children, and then his health
C. He made Nuh's people rebel against him
D. He caused Nuh to forget his prayers
questions.explanation: Iblis, given permission by Allah to test Nuh ﷺ, targeted his blessings by destroying his wealth, children, and health to challenge his faith and patience, demonstrating the trials prophets often face in their mission.

questions.q: What are the six pillars of faith (Eemaan) mentioned in the hadeeth of Jibreel?
opts: A. Prayer, Fasting, Zakaah, Hajj, Shahadah, Jihad
B. Belief in Allaah, angels, books, messengers, Last Day, and Qadar
C. Belief in prophets, saints, martyrs, righteous, angels, and jinn
D. Tawheed, Adl, Nubuwwah, Imamaah, Qiyamah, and Qadar
questions.explanation: The six pillars of faith (Eemaan) outlined in the hadeeth of Jibreel include belief in Allaah, His angels, His revealed books, His messengers, the Last Day (Day of Judgment), and Qadar (divine predestination), which together form the foundation of a Muslim's faith.

questions.q: From which Arabic word does "Nabee" (prophet) come from, and what does it mean?
opts: A. Naba', which means news or information
B. Nabaha, which means to be prominent
C. Nabawah, which means high status
questions.explanation: "Nabee" comes from the Arabic word "Naba'," which means news or information, highlighting that a prophet brings important messages or divine news to people. This emphasizes the role of prophets as conveyors of God's guidance and revelations.

questions.q: What is the meaning of "Rasool" (messenger) in Arabic?
opts: B. A person who carries a "Risaalah" or message
C. A person who leads prayers
D. A person who recites scriptures
questions.explanation: "Rasool" in Arabic means a messenger who is specifically sent by Allah to deliver a divine message or scripture, known as "Risaalah," to guide people. This role involves conveying God's commands and teachings to humanity.

questions.q: According to the text, in how many forms does revelation (wahy) come to a prophet?
questions.explanation: Revelation (wahy) comes to a prophet in three forms: through direct speech from Allah, through inspiration or a sudden strong feeling, and via angelic communication, often by the Angel Gabriel. These methods ensure the message is conveyed clearly and accurately to the prophet.

questions.q: What is the main difference between a prophet (Nabee) and a messenger (Rasool)?
opts: A. A messenger is always an angel, while a prophet is human
B. A messenger receives a new message or law from Allaah, while a prophet teaches an existing message
C. A prophet is greater than a messenger
D. There is no difference; they mean the same thing
questions.explanation: A messenger (Rasool) is sent with a new divine message or law to guide people, while a prophet (Nabee) is sent to reinforce and teach the existing message without bringing a new law. This distinction highlights the unique role of messengers in delivering fresh revelations.

questions.q: Which statement is TRUE according to the relationship between prophets and messengers?
opts: A. Every messenger is a prophet, but not every prophet is a messenger
B. Every prophet is a messenger, but not every messenger is a prophet
C. They are completely separate categories
D. Messengers are only from the Children of Israel
questions.explanation: Messengers (Rasul) are prophets (Nabi) who receive a new scripture or law to convey, while prophets may continue or reinforce existing teachings without bringing a new scripture. Thus, all messengers are prophets, but not all prophets are messengers.

questions.q: What does Allaah say about those who make a distinction between the messengers?
opts: A. They are following the correct path
C. They will be forgiven because they believe in some
D. They are following the Sunnah
questions.explanation: Making a distinction between the messengers implies rejecting some prophets while accepting others, which contradicts the belief in all prophets sent by Allaah. This attitude is considered disbelief because it denies the complete message Allaah revealed through His messengers.

questions.q: According to the text, rejecting some of the prophets is considered:
opts: B. Permissible if you believe in most
questions.explanation: Rejecting some of the prophets goes against the core Islamic belief in all messengers sent by Allah, which is essential for faith. Denying any prophet is seen as disbelief (kufr) because it rejects part of Allah’s guidance to humanity.

questions.q: What did Moosaa (Moses) ask Allaah that was not granted to him?
opts: D. To have his people forgiven
questions.explanation: Moosaa (Moses) asked to see Allaah directly, but Allaah told him that no one can see Him in this life. This teaches that Allah's essence is beyond human perception, emphasizing His transcendence and the limits of human experience.

questions.q: According to Ibn ul-Qayyim, what is the ONLY way to happiness and success in this life and the next?
questions.explanation: Ibn ul-Qayyim teaches that true happiness and success in both this life and the hereafter come only by following the guidance of the messengers, as they convey Allah’s divine message and lead people to the right path. Without their guidance, one cannot achieve ultimate success.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: Who was the first man and the first prophet?
questions.explanation: Aadam (Adam) is considered the first man created by Allah and the first prophet sent to guide humanity. He was given knowledge and the responsibility to teach his descendants about worshiping Allah and living righteously.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: How many years did Aadam (Adam) live according to the hadeeth?
questions.explanation: According to authentic hadeeth, Prophet Aadam (Adam) lived for 960 years. This long lifespan is mentioned to highlight the early human era's extended life spans before the gradual reduction over generations.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: What was the height of Aadam (Adam) according to the hadeeth?
questions.explanation: According to a hadeeth, the height of Prophet Aadam (Adam) was 60 cubits, which is approximately 90 feet. This emphasizes his unique creation and the extraordinary nature of the first human in Islamic tradition.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: Who was the first messenger?
questions.explanation: Nooh (Noah) is considered the first messenger in Islam because he was the first prophet sent by Allah to guide his people towards monotheism and righteousness, warning them of the consequences of disbelief and sin.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: Who is the last prophet and last messenger?
questions.explanation: Muhammad is considered the last prophet and messenger in Islam, known as the "Seal of the Prophets," meaning no prophets will come after him. His teachings complete and finalize the guidance sent by previous prophets.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: According to the hadeeth, how many prophets were sent in total?
questions.explanation: According to Islamic tradition, a total of 124,000 prophets were sent by Allah to guide humanity throughout history. This number highlights the extensive and continuous divine guidance provided to different peoples and nations.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: How many messengers are mentioned in the Qur'aan and Sunnah combined?
questions.explanation: The Qur'aan and Sunnah together mention 33 messengers (Rusul), who were sent by Allah to guide humanity. These messengers delivered divine messages and laws, distinct from the broader category of prophets (Anbiya), some of whom were not messengers.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: How many prophets are named in the Qur'aan?
questions.explanation: The Qur'aan explicitly names 25 prophets, highlighting their stories and messages to guide humanity. These prophets serve as examples of faith and obedience to Allah, each delivering His guidance to different communities throughout history.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: Who are "Uloo al-'Azm" (the messengers of strong will)?
opts: A. The prophets who were kings
B. The five best messengers: Muhammad, Ibraaheem, Moosaa, 'Eesaa, and Nooh
C. The prophets who performed the most miracles
D. The prophets who lived the longest
questions.explanation: 'Uloo al-'Azm' refers to the five greatest prophets known for their strong determination and perseverance in conveying Allah's message: Muhammad, Ibraaheem, Moosaa, 'Eesaa, and Nooh. They faced significant challenges but remained steadfast in their missions.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: What is the meaning of "Al-Asbaat" mentioned in the Qur'aan?
opts: A. The prophets who were also kings
C. The angels who visited prophets
D. The righteous people after Moosaa
questions.explanation: "Al-Asbaat" refers to the twelve sons of Prophet Ya'qoob (Jacob), who became the ancestors of the twelve tribes of Bani Israel. This term highlights their significance in Islamic tradition as the progenitors of these tribes mentioned in the Qur'an.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: According to the text, how many times are Al-Asbaat mentioned in the Qur'aan?
questions.explanation: Al-Asbaat, referring to the twelve tribes descended from Prophet Ya'qub (Jacob), are mentioned five times in the Qur'an to highlight their significance in Islamic history and the continuity of prophetic guidance through their lineage.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: Who are the two prophets mentioned in Soorat al-Kahf without their names being given?
questions.explanation: In Soorat al-Kahf, the two prophets are described through their story but not named directly; Moosaa (Moses) is the seeker of knowledge, and Al-Khaḍir is the wise servant guiding him, illustrating lessons about divine wisdom and patience.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: After Ibraaheem, from whom did all prophets descend?
opts: A. His son Is-haaq (Isaac) and his descendants
D. Both his sons Is-haaq and Ismaa'eel
questions.explanation: All prophets after Ibraaheem (Abraham) descended from his son Is-haaq (Isaac) and his descendants, as this lineage continued the prophetic tradition through the children of Israel, fulfilling Allah’s plan to guide humanity through chosen messengers from this family line.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: According to the hadeeth, how many years were between Aadam and Nooh?
questions.explanation: According to the hadeeth, there were 10 centuries, or 1,000 years, between Prophet Aadam and Prophet Nooh. This timeframe highlights the long span of human history and the gradual development of prophetic guidance over generations.

questions.topic: The Prophets · Who Are the Prophets
questions.q: Which prophet is known as "Thun-Noon" (the man of the fish)?
questions.explanation: Prophet Yoonus (Jonah) is called "Thun-Noon" because he was swallowed by a large fish or whale as a test of faith and patience. This story highlights his repentance and God's mercy, making him known as the "man of the fish" in Islamic tradition.

questions.q: To which people was Prophet Hud sent?
questions.explanation: Prophet Hud ﷺ was sent to the people of 'Ad, an ancient tribe known for their strength and arrogance. He called them to worship Allah alone and abandon idolatry, but they largely rejected his message, leading to their eventual destruction as a divine punishment.

questions.q: Where did the people of 'Ad live?
opts: C. In Al-Ahqaf between Yemen and Oman
questions.explanation: The people of 'Ad lived in Al-Ahqaf, a region characterized by sandy dunes located between Yemen and Oman. This area is known for its harsh desert environment, where the tribe of 'Ad was granted strength and prosperity before their eventual punishment for rejecting Prophet Hud's message.

questions.q: What punishment was sent to destroy the people of 'Ad?
opts: B. A furious violent wind for seven nights and eight days
D. Being swallowed by the earth
questions.explanation: The people of 'Ad were destroyed by a furious, violent wind that lasted for seven nights and eight days, as a divine punishment for their persistent disbelief and arrogance. This severe wind demolished their homes and left them powerless against Allah's decree.

questions.q: How did the disbelievers among 'Ad react when they saw a cloud approaching?
opts: A. They recognized it as punishment and repented
B. They thought it was a rain cloud bringing them water
C. They hid in their homes
questions.explanation: The disbelievers among 'Ad saw the approaching cloud and mistakenly believed it was a rain cloud that would bring them water, showing their misunderstanding of the warning signs sent by Allah. Instead, it was a destructive storm meant as a punishment for their disbelief.

questions.q: What did Hud tell his people when they asked him to bring the punishment?
opts: A. That he would ask Allah to delay it
B. That only Allah would bring it, and they would not escape
C. That the punishment would never come
D. That they should flee to the mountains
questions.explanation: Hud ﷺ told his people that the punishment was solely in Allah’s control and that no one could avoid it if it was decreed. This emphasized that human efforts cannot prevent divine judgment, highlighting the importance of faith and obedience to Allah.

questions.q: To which people was Prophet Salih sent?
questions.explanation: Prophet Salih ﷺ was sent to the people of Thamud, who lived in the Arabian Peninsula. They were known for carving homes out of mountains, and Salih called them to worship Allah alone and abandon their sinful ways.

questions.q: What miracle did the people of Thamud demand from Salih?
opts: A. That he bring water from a rock
B. That a unique she-camel issue from the mountains
C. That he raise the dead
D. That he turn their mountains into gold
questions.explanation: The people of Thamud demanded a miracle from Prophet Salih ﷺ to prove his prophethood, specifically that a unique she-camel would emerge from the mountains as a sign from Allah. This she-camel was a test of their faith and obedience to God's command.

questions.q: What warning did Salih give his people about the she-camel?
opts: B. To leave it to graze freely and not harm it
C. To sacrifice it to Allah
D. To keep it in a special temple
questions.explanation: Salih ﷺ warned his people to let the she-camel graze freely and not harm it, as it was a miraculous sign from Allah. Hurting the camel would bring severe consequences, emphasizing the importance of obedience and respect for divine signs.

questions.q: What happened to the she-camel?
opts: A. It lived among them peacefully
B. It was killed by nine men who plotted against it
C. It returned to the mountain
D. It was taken by another tribe
questions.explanation: The she-camel sent as a miracle to Prophet Salih’s people was killed by nine men who conspired against it, defying God's warning. This act led to the punishment and destruction of the people for their disobedience and rejection of the prophet’s message.

questions.q: How long did Salih tell the killers they had to enjoy life before punishment?
questions.explanation: Prophet Salih ﷺ warned the people of Thamud that they had only three days to enjoy life before Allah’s punishment would befall them, emphasizing the urgency of repentance and the consequences of their wrongdoing.

questions.q: What punishment destroyed the people of Thamud?
opts: B. Earthquake and a terrible cry
D. Being swallowed by the earth
questions.explanation: The people of Thamud were destroyed by a severe earthquake combined with a dreadful, overwhelming sound as a divine punishment for rejecting Prophet Salih ﷺ and his message. This event is mentioned in the Quran to illustrate the consequences of disobedience to Allah's guidance.

questions.q: What was the name of Ibrahim's father according to some traditions?
questions.explanation: According to some Islamic traditions, Ibrahim's ﷺ father was named Tarikh or Azer, reflecting variations in historical and cultural sources. These names highlight the different narrations about his lineage, emphasizing the importance of understanding diverse accounts within Islamic history.

questions.q: What did Ibrahim do when his people left the city for a festival?
opts: B. He stayed home to pray
C. He went to the temple and broke all the idols except the biggest one
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ broke all the idols except the largest one to show his people that idols have no power and to challenge their belief, demonstrating that the biggest idol could not have destroyed the others, emphasizing the futility of idol worship.

questions.q: How did Ibrahim reason with the idolaters when they questioned him about breaking the idols?
opts: A. He confessed and asked for forgiveness
B. He suggested they ask the biggest idol who did it
C. He blamed the other idols
D. He said he didn't know what happened
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ used logic to show the idolaters the futility of worshipping idols by suggesting they ask the largest idol who broke the others, highlighting that idols have no power or ability to act, thus proving they are not worthy of worship.

questions.q: What command did Allah give to the fire when Ibrahim was thrown into it?
opts: B. "Be you coolness and safety for Ibrahim"
D. "Rise up and destroy his enemies"
questions.explanation: When Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ was thrown into the fire, Allah commanded the fire to be cool and safe for him, protecting him from harm. This miraculous event demonstrated Allah’s power to save His prophets and showed Ibrahim’s unwavering faith.

questions.q: Who was the king who disputed with Ibrahim about Allah and claimed to give life and cause death?
questions.explanation: Nimrod was a powerful king who challenged Prophet Ibrahim's belief in one God by claiming he could give life and cause death, asserting his own authority over life and death instead of Allah's. This story highlights the contrast between divine power and human arrogance.

questions.q: According to the hadith, how many times did Ibrahim tell a lie, and for what purpose?
opts: B. Three times, all for the sake of Allah
C. Five times, to save himself
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ told a lie three times, each to protect his life or others, and all were done sincerely for the sake of Allah. This shows that in rare cases, lying can be permissible if it serves a higher moral or divine purpose.

questions.q: What did the tyrant king give to Sarah when he realized she was protected by Allah?
opts: B. Hajar as a maid servant
questions.explanation: When the tyrant king saw that Sarah was protected by Allah and could not be harmed, he gave her Hajar as a maid servant to assist her. This was a common practice to provide companionship and support within noble households.

questions.q: What did Allah command Ibrahim to do with four birds to demonstrate how He brings the dead to life?
opts: A. Release them in four directions
B. Cut them up, mix their parts, place them on hills, and call them back
C. Sacrifice them and burn them
D. Keep them in a cage
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ was commanded to cut four birds into pieces, place the parts on different hills, and then call them to come back to him. This miracle demonstrated Allah’s power to bring the dead back to life, showing that resurrection is possible by His will.

questions.q: Where did Ibrahim leave Hajar and Isma'il by Allah's command?
opts: B. In a barren valley near the site of the Ka'ba in Mecca
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ left Hajar and Isma'il in a barren valley near the future site of the Ka'ba in Mecca as part of Allah's divine plan, testing their faith and patience. This location later became a central place of worship and pilgrimage in Islam.

questions.q: What did Hajar do when the water ran out and her child became thirsty?
opts: B. She ran seven times between Safa and Marwah searching for water
questions.explanation: When the water ran out and her son Isma'il became thirsty, Hajar ran seven times between the hills of Safa and Marwah searching for water, demonstrating her faith and determination. This event is commemorated in the Islamic ritual of Sa'i during Hajj and Umrah.

questions.q: What instruction did Ibrahim give through his message to Isma'il's first wife?
opts: B. To change the threshold of the gate (divorce her)
C. To move to a new location
questions.explanation: Ibrahim instructed Isma'il to change the threshold of the gate, which was a symbolic way of telling him to divorce his first wife, as part of fulfilling a divine command and ensuring the proper lineage and legacy for Isma'il's descendants.

questions.q: What did Ibrahim and Isma'il say while building the foundations of the Ka'ba?
opts: B. "Our Lord! Accept this service from us, verily You are the All-Hearer, the All-Knower"
C. "We have built this house for ourselves"
questions.explanation: Ibrahim and Isma'il prayed to Allah while building the Ka'ba, asking Him to accept their work as an act of worship, acknowledging that Allah hears and knows all. This shows their devotion and reliance on Allah's acceptance in their righteous efforts.

questions.q: What did Allah command Ibrahim to sacrifice in a dream?
questions.explanation: Allah commanded Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ in a dream to sacrifice his son Isma'il ﷺ as a test of faith and obedience. This event signifies complete submission to Allah's will and is commemorated annually during Eid al-Adha.

questions.q: What was Isma'il's response when Ibrahim told him about the dream of sacrificing him?
opts: B. He said: "O my father! Do that which you are commanded, you shall find me of the patient"
C. He asked to be spared
questions.explanation: Isma'il ﷺ showed complete trust and submission to Allah's command by encouraging his father Ibrahim ﷺ to fulfill the divine instruction, demonstrating his patience and faith in God's wisdom during the test of sacrifice.

questions.q: What did Allah provide to ransom Isma'il?
questions.explanation: When Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ was about to sacrifice his son Isma'il ﷺ in obedience to Allah's command, Allah provided a ram to be sacrificed instead, demonstrating His mercy and the importance of submission to His will.

questions.q: Who did Isaac marry?
questions.explanation: Isaac married Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel, as mentioned in Islamic tradition and the Bible. Their marriage was arranged by Abraham's servant, symbolizing a union chosen for piety and family lineage.

questions.q: What twin sons did Isaac and Rebekah have?
questions.explanation: Isaac (Ishaq) and Rebekah had twin sons named Esau and Jacob, who are significant figures in Islamic tradition. Jacob (Ya'qub) is especially noted as a prophet, while Esau is recognized as his brother. Their story highlights themes of faith, destiny, and family in Islamic teachings.

questions.q: At what age did Isaac die according to the text?
questions.explanation: Isaac (Ishaq ﷺ) is reported in Islamic tradition and some historical texts to have lived for 180 years, reflecting his long life as a prophet and patriarch. This age signifies his enduring role in the lineage of prophets.

questions.q: Where was Isaac buried?
opts: B. In a cave with his father Abraham in Hebron
questions.explanation: Isaac ﷺ was buried in the Cave of the Patriarchs in Hebron, alongside his father Abraham ﷺ. This site is significant as a family burial place for several prophets and is revered in Islamic tradition.

questions.q: What does the name Israel (Yaqub's other name) mean?
opts: D. Belonging to the people of Israel
questions.explanation: The name Israel means "one who struggles with God" or "God contends," reflecting Yaqub's (Jacob's) spiritual perseverance. It also signifies the progenitor of the Israelites, thus "belonging to the people of Israel" refers to his role as their ancestor.

questions.q: Why did Jacob leave his home and go to his uncle Laban?
opts: B. Because his brother Esau threatened to kill him
questions.explanation: Jacob left his home and went to his uncle Laban because his brother Esau was angry and threatened to kill him after Jacob received their father Isaac's blessing. This move ensured Jacob's safety and allowed him to find a wife from his family.

questions.q: How many years did Jacob work for Laban to marry his daughters?
opts: B. Fourteen years (seven for each daughter)
questions.explanation: Jacob worked for Laban for fourteen years in total, seven years for each daughter, Leah and Rachel, as a condition to marry them. This demonstrates his patience and dedication in fulfilling his commitment to Laban.

questions.q: How many sons did Jacob have?
questions.explanation: Jacob ﷺ had twelve sons, who became the ancestors of the twelve tribes of Israel. This is significant in Islamic and biblical traditions, symbolizing the foundation of a large and important community.

questions.q: Who was the favorite son of Jacob?
questions.explanation: Joseph was the favorite son of Jacob because he was born to Jacob’s beloved wife Rachel and showed exceptional qualities, which made Jacob love him more than his other sons. This favoritism is mentioned in the Quran and the Bible, highlighting Joseph’s special status in Jacob’s family.

questions.q: What did Jacob advise his sons on his deathbed?
opts: B. To worship Allah alone and die as Muslims
C. To return to their homeland
questions.explanation: Jacob ﷺ advised his sons to remain faithful to Allah alone, emphasizing monotheism and encouraging them to live and die as Muslims, upholding the worship of the One true God without associating partners. This reflects the core message of all prophets to maintain pure faith.

questions.q: What did Joseph see in his dream?
opts: A. Eleven stars, the sun, and the moon prostrating to him
B. A ladder reaching to heaven
D. His brothers bowing to him
questions.explanation: Joseph saw eleven stars, the sun, and the moon prostrating to him in his dream, symbolizing his eleven brothers, his father, and his mother showing respect and submission, which foretold his future rise to a position of honor and authority over his family.

questions.q: What did Jacob advise Joseph about his dream?
opts: A. To tell everyone about it
B. Not to relate it to his brothers lest they plot against him
D. To ask Allah for its interpretation
questions.explanation: Jacob advised Joseph not to share his dream with his brothers because he feared they might become jealous and harm him. This caution was to protect Joseph from potential harm due to his brothers' envy.

questions.q: What did Joseph's brothers do to him?
opts: B. They threw him into a well and later sold him as a slave
C. They kept him as a servant
D. They sent him to another city
questions.explanation: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him and plotted against him by throwing him into a well. Later, they sold him as a slave to traders, which led to his journey to Egypt and eventual rise to power. This story teaches about patience and trust in God's plan.

questions.q: Who bought Joseph in Egypt?
questions.explanation: Joseph ﷺ was sold into slavery and eventually bought by Al-Aziz, the chief minister of Egypt, who recognized his wisdom and integrity. This position allowed Joseph to rise to prominence and fulfill his role as a prophet and leader in Egypt.

questions.q: What did the chief minister's wife (Zulaikha) try to do?
opts: D. Marry Joseph to her daughter
questions.explanation: Zulaikha, the chief minister's wife, tried to seduce Prophet Yusuf (Joseph) because she was captivated by his beauty and virtue. Her attempt is a significant event in his story, highlighting his steadfastness and commitment to resisting temptation.

questions.q: How was Joseph's innocence proven when Zulaikha accused him?
opts: B. His shirt was torn from the back
questions.explanation: Joseph's innocence was proven because his shirt was torn from the back, indicating he was fleeing from Zulaikha rather than attacking her, which confirmed he did not attempt to harm or seduce her.

questions.q: What did the women of the city do when they saw Joseph?
opts: B. They cut their hands in astonishment at his beauty
questions.explanation: When the women of the city saw Prophet Yusuf's ﷺ extraordinary beauty, they were so amazed that they unintentionally cut their hands while trying to touch him, showing how striking his appearance was. This event highlights Yusuf's ﷺ exceptional physical beauty mentioned in the Quran.

questions.q: What ability did Allah grant Joseph in prison?
questions.explanation: Allah granted Prophet Yusuf (Joseph) the special ability to interpret dreams while he was in prison, which helped him understand the meanings of the dreams of his fellow prisoners and later played a key role in his rise to power. This gift was a sign of Allah’s wisdom and support.

questions.q: What did the king's dream of seven fat cows and seven lean cows symbolize?
opts: A. Seven years of war and seven years of peace
B. Seven years of abundance followed by seven years of famine
C. Seven years of happiness and seven years of sorrow
questions.explanation: The seven fat cows represented seven years of plentiful harvests, while the seven lean cows symbolized seven years of severe famine. This dream warned the king to prepare for future scarcity by storing surplus grain during the years of abundance.

questions.q: What condition did Joseph make before leaving prison?
opts: A. That he be given wealth
B. That his innocence be proven regarding the women who cut their hands
C. That he be made king
D. That his brothers be brought to him
questions.explanation: Joseph ﷺ requested that his innocence be established before leaving prison to clear his name from the false accusation made by the women who cut their hands, ensuring justice and protecting his honor. This condition highlighted his commitment to truth and integrity.

questions.q: When Joseph's brothers came to Egypt for grain, did they recognize him?
opts: B. No, they did not recognize him
C. They suspected who he was
questions.explanation: Joseph's brothers did not recognize him when they came to Egypt for grain because many years had passed, and Joseph's appearance had changed. Additionally, Joseph deliberately concealed his identity to test their character and repentance.

questions.q: How did Joseph make his brothers bring Benjamin to Egypt?
opts: B. He kept one brother as a hostage and told them to bring Benjamin as proof
C. He sent a letter to Jacob
D. He offered them more grain
questions.explanation: Joseph kept one brother, Simeon, as a hostage to ensure the others would return with their youngest brother, Benjamin, proving their honesty and fulfilling his plan to reunite with his family. This strategy was meant to test their loyalty and repentance.

questions.q: How did Joseph keep Benjamin in Egypt?
opts: B. He had his silver cup placed in Benjamin's bag and accused him of theft
D. He offered him a position
questions.explanation: Joseph secretly placed his silver cup in Benjamin's bag and then accused him of stealing it to test his brothers' honesty and loyalty, ultimately revealing his identity and reuniting with his family.

questions.q: What did Jacob say when he smelled Joseph's shirt?
opts: B. "I do indeed feel the smell of Joseph"
questions.explanation: When Jacob smelled Joseph's shirt, he recognized its scent and expressed his certainty by saying, "I do indeed feel the smell of Joseph," showing his deep hope and faith that his son was still alive despite years of separation.

questions.q: What did Joseph say to his brothers when they finally recognized him?
opts: A. "I will punish you for what you did"
B. "No reproach on you this day, may Allah forgive you"
D. "You must pay for your crimes"
questions.explanation: When Joseph ﷺ revealed himself to his brothers, he showed forgiveness and mercy by saying, "No reproach on you this day, may Allah forgive you," demonstrating the importance of forgiveness and trusting in Allah’s mercy even after being wronged.

questions.q: How did Allah describe Prophet Ayyub in the Quran?
opts: A. "How excellent a slave! Verily, he was ever of returning in repentance to Us"
questions.explanation: Allah described Prophet Ayyub ﷺ as an excellent servant who was always turning back to Him in repentance, highlighting his patience and steadfastness despite severe trials. This praise emphasizes his unwavering faith and constant seeking of Allah’s mercy.

questions.q: How did Allah restore Ayyub's health?
opts: A. By sending an angel to heal him
B. By commanding him to strike the ground with his foot, and a spring of water appeared
D. By sending a dream with instructions
questions.explanation: Allah commanded Prophet Ayyub ﷺ to strike the ground with his foot, causing a spring of water to appear. Drinking from this spring healed him, demonstrating Allah’s mercy and power to restore health after severe trials.

questions.q: How did Ayyub fulfill his oath to punish his wife with a hundred strokes without hurting her?
opts: A. He forgave her and broke the oath
B. He struck her once lightly and Allah counted it as a hundred
C. He took a bundle of thin grass and struck her with it
questions.explanation: Ayyub ﷺ fulfilled his oath by using a bundle of thin grass to strike his wife, ensuring he did not cause her harm while keeping his promise. This shows his commitment to both his oath and compassion.

questions.q: To which people was Prophet Shu'aib sent?
questions.explanation: Prophet Shu'aib ﷺ was sent to the people of Madyan (Midian) to guide them towards monotheism and honest trade, warning against corruption and dishonesty. His mission emphasized justice and ethical behavior in both worship and daily dealings.

questions.q: What were the major sins of the people of Madyan?
opts: B. Giving short measure, cheating in weights, and causing mischief
questions.explanation: The people of Madyan were known for dishonest trade practices like giving short measure and cheating in weights, which are major sins in Islam. They also caused mischief and corruption on the earth, prompting Prophet Shu'aib ﷺ to warn them to be just and fair.

questions.q: What did the people of Madyan threaten Shu'aib with?
opts: B. Driving him and his followers out of the town
questions.explanation: The people of Madyan threatened Shu'aib ﷺ with driving him and his followers out of the town because they rejected his call to worship Allah alone and to be honest in trade. They wanted to maintain their corrupt practices and opposed his message of justice and monotheism.

questions.q: What punishment destroyed the people of Madyan?
opts: C. Scorching heat followed by a thunderous sound and fire from a cloud
D. Being swallowed by the earth
questions.explanation: The people of Madyan were destroyed by a severe punishment involving scorching heat, followed by a thunderous sound and fire from a cloud, as a consequence of their persistent disbelief and wrongdoing despite Prophet Shu'aib's ﷺ warnings. This divine punishment served as a lesson for others to heed prophetic guidance.

questions.q: Why did Pharaoh order the killing of male children among the Israelites?
opts: B. He saw in a vision that a boy from Israel would cause his downfall
questions.explanation: Pharaoh ordered the killing of male Israelite children because he feared a prophecy or vision that a boy from the Israelites would grow up to overthrow him, threatening his rule and power. This was an attempt to prevent the predicted downfall by eliminating potential threats early.

questions.q: What did Allah inspire Moses's mother to do when she feared for his life?
opts: A. Hide him in the house
B. Put him in a basket and cast him into the river
C. Send him to another city
D. Give him to the Egyptians
questions.explanation: When Moses's mother feared for his life due to Pharaoh's decree, Allah inspired her to place him in a basket and set it afloat on the river, trusting in Allah's protection. This act ensured Moses's safety and eventual rescue by Pharaoh's family.

questions.q: Who found Moses in the basket?
questions.explanation: Moses was found in a basket floating on the river by the household of Pharaoh, who then took him in and raised him. This event is significant as it marks the beginning of Moses' life in the palace before his prophethood.

questions.q: Why did Moses refuse to take milk from any wet nurse?
opts: B. Allah had forbidden other foster mothers so that his real mother could nurse him
D. He only wanted his mother's milk
questions.explanation: Moses refused milk from any wet nurse because Allah had commanded that only his real mother should nurse him, ensuring a special bond and protection as part of divine wisdom in his upbringing. This was to fulfill Allah’s decree and safeguard Moses during his early life.

questions.q: What did Moses do that caused him to flee from Egypt?
opts: B. He accidentally killed an Egyptian who was fighting with an Israelite
D. He refused to worship the Egyptian gods
questions.explanation: Moses fled Egypt after he unintentionally killed an Egyptian while defending an Israelite, fearing punishment from Pharaoh. This event led him to escape to Midian, where he later received his prophetic mission.

questions.q: How long did Moses stay in Midian?
questions.explanation: Moses stayed in Midian for ten years, during which he served his father-in-law, Prophet Shuayb (Jethro). This period was important for Moses to gain experience and prepare for his mission to free the Israelites.

questions.q: What did Moses see at Mount Tur that led to him receiving prophethood?
opts: A. A burning bush that was not consumed
B. A fire in the distance
questions.explanation: Moses saw a fire on Mount Tur, which drew his attention and led him to approach it. This encounter was the moment Allah spoke to him, marking the beginning of his prophethood and the mission to guide the Children of Israel.

questions.q: What two miracles did Allah give to Moses?
opts: A. His staff turning into a snake and his hand becoming white when placed in his armpit
B. Parting the sea and bringing water from a rock
C. Healing the sick and raising the dead
D. Controlling the wind and talking to animals
questions.explanation: Allah granted Moses two signs: his staff turning into a snake to demonstrate divine power, and his hand becoming shining white when placed under his armpit to show a clear, miraculous sign to Pharaoh and the people. These miracles confirmed his prophethood and Allah’s support.

questions.q: Who did Moses ask Allah to appoint as his helper?
questions.explanation: Moses asked Allah to appoint his brother Aaron as his helper because Aaron was eloquent in speech and could assist in conveying the message to Pharaoh and the people, making their mission more effective.

questions.q: How did the magicians of Pharaoh react when Moses's staff swallowed their ropes and sticks?
opts: B. They fell down prostrate, believing in the Lord of Moses and Aaron
questions.explanation: When Moses's staff swallowed the magicians' ropes and sticks, it demonstrated a clear miracle from Allah, leading the magicians to recognize the divine power and submit by prostrating, affirming their belief in the Lord of Moses and Aaron.

questions.q: What did Pharaoh threaten to do to the magicians who believed?
opts: B. Cut off their hands and feet and crucify them
questions.explanation: Pharaoh threatened to cut off the hands and feet of the magicians on opposite sides and crucify them because they believed in the message of Musa (Moses) and Harun (Aaron) ﷺ, which challenged his authority and threatened his control over the people. This severe punishment was meant to intimidate and prevent others from following them.

questions.q: What were the five signs (plagues) sent upon the Egyptians?
opts: A. Flood, locusts, lice, frogs, and blood
B. Darkness, boils, hail, fire, and death
C. Drought, famine, disease, storms, and snakes
D. Earthquakes, fire, smoke, darkness, and blood
questions.explanation: The five signs sent upon the Egyptians were divine plagues to persuade Pharaoh to release the Israelites. These included a flood, locusts, lice, frogs, and blood, each demonstrating Allah’s power and challenging the Egyptian gods and Pharaoh’s authority.

questions.q: What happened to the Red Sea when Moses struck it with his staff?
opts: B. It parted, creating a dry path
questions.explanation: When Moses struck the Red Sea with his staff, Allah caused the waters to part, creating a dry path for the Israelites to safely cross and escape Pharaoh's pursuing army. This miraculous event demonstrated Allah’s power and helped save the believers.

questions.q: What did Pharaoh say when he was drowning?
opts: A. "I am the lord of the universe"
B. "I believe that there is no god worthy of worship except Allah in Whom the children of Israel believe"
questions.explanation: Pharaoh declared his belief in the one true God, Allah, as he was drowning, acknowledging the faith of the children of Israel. This moment shows that even those who opposed the prophets can recognize the truth at the end.

questions.q: What did the Israelites ask Moses to make for them when they saw idol worshippers?
opts: B. A god like the idols they saw
questions.explanation: The Israelites asked Moses to make a god like the idols they saw because they were influenced by idol worship and wanted a tangible object to worship, despite Moses teaching them to worship the one true God alone. This reflects their struggle with faith and reliance on visible symbols.

questions.q: What food did Allah provide for the Israelites in the wilderness?
questions.explanation: Allah provided the Israelites with manna, a sweet, flaky substance, and quails, a type of bird, as miraculous food during their journey in the wilderness to sustain them when other provisions were scarce.

questions.q: How long did Moses fast before going to Mount Sinai to receive the tablets?
opts: B. Thirty days, then an additional ten days (forty total)
questions.explanation: Moses fasted for thirty days and then an additional ten days, totaling forty days, before ascending Mount Sinai to receive the tablets. This period of fasting and seclusion was a time of spiritual preparation and closeness to Allah.

questions.q: What did the Israelites worship in Moses's absence?
opts: A. A golden calf made by Samiri
questions.explanation: During Moses's absence on Mount Sinai, the Israelites were led astray by Samiri, who crafted a golden calf for them to worship, violating the commandment to worship Allah alone. This act represented a major lapse in faith and obedience among the Israelites.

questions.q: What punishment was imposed on those who worshipped the calf?
opts: B. They had to kill themselves (the innocent kill the wrongdoers)
questions.explanation: Those who worshipped the calf were commanded to repent and face consequences, where the innocent were instructed to confront the wrongdoers, leading to a form of self-punishment among the guilty. This was meant to purify the community and restore monotheism as taught by Prophet Musa ﷺ.

questions.q: What did the seventy elders demand when they went with Moses to Mount Sinai?
opts: D. To have Aaron as their leader
questions.explanation: The seventy elders demanded to see Allah plainly when they accompanied Moses to Mount Sinai, seeking a direct vision of God. However, Allah informed them that no one can see Him directly in this life, emphasizing His transcendence and the limits of human perception.

questions.q: What happened to the man who was killed, and how was the murderer identified through the cow?
opts: A. The cow spoke and named the murderer
B. The dead man was struck with a piece of the cow and was brought back to life, revealing the murderer
C. The cow led them to the murderer
D. The murderer confessed when he saw the cow
questions.explanation: When the dead man was struck with a piece of the cow, he was miraculously brought back to life, which allowed him to identify his murderer. This event demonstrated Allah’s power and served as a divine means to reveal the truth.

questions.q: Who did Moses meet at the junction of the two seas?
questions.explanation: Moses met Al-Khidr at the junction of the two seas, where Al-Khidr imparted hidden wisdom and lessons through seemingly unusual actions, teaching Moses about divine knowledge beyond his own understanding.
