questions.q: Who was Shayth (Seth) and what was his significance?
opts: B. He was the righteous son given to Adam after Habil's death, and he became a prophet
C. He was Adam's twin brother
D. He was an angel sent to help Adam
questions.explanation: Shayth (Seth) was the son of Prophet Adam ﷺ, born after the death of Habil (Abel). He is significant because he continued Adam’s prophetic lineage and was known for his righteousness, guiding people towards monotheism and obedience to Allah.

questions.q: How did the people of Thamud respond to Salih's message?
opts: B. They asked for the miracle, then after it came, most disbelieved and demanded Salih bring the punishment
C. They accepted the camel as their god
questions.explanation: The people of Thamud initially challenged Prophet Salih ﷺ by demanding a miracle as proof of his prophethood. After the she-camel miracle was granted, most still rejected his message and arrogantly demanded that the promised punishment be sent upon them, showing their persistent disbelief and arrogance.

questions.q: What words of repentance did Adam learn from his Lord?
opts: B. "Rabbana zalamna anfusana wa in lam taghfir lana wa tarhamna lanakunanna min al-khasireen"
D. "La ilaha illa anta subhanaka inni kuntu min al-thalimeen"
questions.explanation: Adam ﷺ was taught the words of repentance, "Rabbana zalamna anfusana wa in lam taghfir lana wa tarhamna lanakunanna min al-khasireen," meaning "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers," showing sincere acknowledgment of sin and seeking Allah’s mercy.

questions.q: What was entrusted to Shayth by Adam?
opts: B. Adam's knowledge, prophethood, and the instructions for future generations
questions.explanation: Shayth (Seth) was entrusted by Adam ﷺ with his knowledge, prophethood, and guidance to pass on instructions for future generations, ensuring the continuation of divine wisdom and guidance after Adam's time.

questions.q: Who killed the she-camel?
opts: B. A man named Qudar ibn Salif, with the support of nine other evildoers
questions.explanation: A man named Qudar ibn Salif, along with nine others, killed the she-camel sent by Prophet Salih ﷺ as a divine sign. This act of defiance led to the punishment of their community, emphasizing the consequences of rejecting God's messengers and miracles.

questions.q: Did Allah accept Adam's repentance?
opts: B. Yes, Allah accepted his repentance and guided him
D. After Adam was sent to earth
questions.explanation: Allah accepted Adam's repentance after he sincerely sought forgiveness for his mistake, demonstrating His mercy and willingness to forgive. This shows that even prophets are human and can err, but sincere repentance leads to Allah's guidance and forgiveness.

questions.q: What happened to humanity between the time of Adam and Nuh (Noah)?
opts: B. They gradually fell into corruption and idolatry until Allah sent Noah
questions.explanation: After Adam, humanity initially followed Allah's guidance but over generations gradually engaged in corruption and idol worship. This moral decline prompted Allah to send Prophet Nuh (Noah) to call people back to monotheism and righteousness.

questions.q: What punishment did Allah send upon the people of Thamud after they killed the camel?
opts: B. A single great blast (sayhah) that destroyed them
questions.explanation: After the people of Thamud killed the miraculous camel sent as a sign, Allah punished them with a single great blast (sayhah) that destroyed them instantly, demonstrating the severe consequences of disobedience and disbelief.

questions.q: When did the punishment come?
opts: B. After three days, during which they awaited it
questions.explanation: The punishment came after a waiting period of three days, during which the people anticipated the consequences of their actions. This delay served as a final warning and a chance for reflection before the punishment was carried out.

questions.q: What is Prophet Ibrahim's title?
questions.explanation: Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ is called Khalilullah, meaning "Friend of Allah," because of his close and sincere relationship with God, marked by unwavering faith and obedience. This title highlights his special status among prophets for his devotion and trust in Allah.

questions.q: What was the occupation of Ibrahim's father (or uncle) Azar?
opts: C. A maker and seller of idols
questions.explanation: Azar, the father (or uncle) of Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ, was known for making and selling idols, which was a common practice in their society. Ibrahim ﷺ challenged this idolatry, calling people to worship the one true God instead.

questions.q: How did Ibrahim first reason against idol worship?
opts: B. By observing the stars, moon, and sun, realizing they set and are not worthy of worship
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ reasoned against idol worship by observing natural phenomena like stars, the moon, and the sun, noting that they rise and set, which means they are transient and cannot be gods. This logical reflection led him to understand that only the eternal Creator deserves worship.

questions.q: What did Ibrahim do to the idols in the temple?
opts: B. He broke them all except the largest, and placed the axe on the largest idol
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ broke all the idols in the temple except the largest one to demonstrate that idols have no power to protect themselves, placing the axe on the biggest idol to show that it could not have done it, thereby proving the futility of idol worship.

questions.q: When the people asked Ibrahim who did this, what did he say?
opts: B. "Ask the largest idol; if he can speak"
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ used this response to show the people the futility of worshipping idols, as they cannot speak or defend themselves, highlighting the need to worship the One true God who is all-powerful and alive.

questions.q: How did the people punish Ibrahim?
opts: B. They threw him into a great fire
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ was punished by being thrown into a great fire because he rejected idol worship and called people to monotheism, which angered his people. However, Allah miraculously saved him by making the fire cool and safe for him.

questions.q: According to the hadith about the "Lammah" (inclination) of the angel and the devil, what is the devil's inclination?
opts: A. To make promises of poverty and to command evil
B. To encourage prayer and fasting
C. To remind people of death
questions.explanation: The devil's inclination is to make false promises of poverty and to command evil actions, aiming to mislead people away from righteousness. This contrasts with the angel's inclination, which encourages promises of wealth and commands good deeds, guiding believers toward positive behavior.

questions.q: What did Allah command the fire to do?
opts: B. To be cool and safe for Ibrahim
D. To spread throughout the city
questions.explanation: Allah commanded the fire to be cool and safe for Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ as a miraculous protection when he was thrown into it, demonstrating Allah’s power to save His prophets from harm. This event highlights Allah’s mercy and the fulfillment of His promise to protect His faithful servants.

questions.q: What does the hadith say about the angel's inclination?
opts: A. To make promises of wealth
B. To promise good and affirm the truth
C. To encourage sleeping and resting
questions.explanation: The hadith explains that angels naturally incline towards promising good and affirming the truth, reflecting their pure and obedient nature in serving Allah. They do not deceive or lie, highlighting their role as trustworthy messengers and helpers in conveying divine guidance.

questions.q: Which angel offered to help Ibrahim in the fire?
opts: D. The Angel of the Mountains
questions.explanation: Angel Jibril (Gabriel) is known for assisting Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ when he was thrown into the fire by his people, as Allah commanded Jibril to cool the fire, ensuring Ibrahim’s safety and demonstrating Allah’s protection over His prophets.

questions.q: From what stages of materials was Adam created, as detailed in the Quranic verses cited by Ibn Kathir?
opts: B. From clay, then sticky mud (teen laazib), then dried clay (salsal) like pottery, then breathed into
C. From dust blown by the wind
D. From a single drop of fluid
questions.explanation: Adam ﷺ was created in stages as described in the Quran and explained by Ibn Kathir: first from clay, then sticky mud (teen laazib), followed by dried clay (salsal) resembling pottery, and finally Allah breathed His spirit into him, giving him life and consciousness.

questions.q: How did Ibrahim respond to the angel's offer?
opts: B. "As for you, no; as for Allah, yes" meaning his trust was in Allah alone
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ rejected the angel's offer because he placed his complete trust in Allah alone, not in any created being. His response, "As for you, no; as for Allah, yes," shows his reliance solely on divine guidance and support.

questions.q: When Allah informed the angels that He was creating a vicegerent (khalifah) on earth, what was their response?
opts: B. They asked "Will You place therein one who will spread corruption and shed blood while we glorify You?"
D. They asked to be sent instead
questions.explanation: The angels questioned Allah about creating a vicegerent because they foresaw human tendencies toward corruption and violence, contrasting with their own constant glorification of Allah. This highlights the unique challenges and responsibilities given to humans on earth.

questions.q: Who was Ibrahim's wife who accompanied him on his journeys?
opts: C. Both a and b - Sarah was his first wife, Hajirah was the second
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ had two wives who accompanied him on different journeys: Sarah, his first wife, stayed with him in Canaan, while Hajirah (Hagar) traveled with him to Mecca, where she cared for their son Ismail. Both played important roles in his life and legacy.

questions.q: How did Allah respond to the angels' question about creating Adam?
opts: A. He explained that Adam would have prophets among his descendants
B. He said "I know that which you do not know"
C. He became angry and silenced them
D. He created Adam immediately to prove them wrong
questions.explanation: When the angels questioned Allah about creating Adam, He responded, "I know that which you do not know," indicating His infinite wisdom and knowledge beyond human and angelic understanding. This shows that Allah's decisions are based on knowledge that is beyond our comprehension.

questions.q: What was the name of Ibrahim's first son, born from Hajirah?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim's first son, born to Hajirah (Hagar), was Isma'il (Ishmael). He is an important prophet in Islam and is considered the ancestor of the Arab people.

questions.q: What did Allah teach Adam that He did not teach the angels, thereby demonstrating Adam's superiority?
opts: A. The names of all things
D. The stories of the past prophets
questions.explanation: Allah taught Adam the names of all things, which the angels did not know, demonstrating Adam's unique knowledge and superiority. This knowledge symbolized human capacity for learning and understanding beyond the angels' abilities.

questions.q: What was the name of Ibrahim's second son, born from Sarah?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim's second son, born to his wife Sarah, was named Ishaq (Isaac). He is a significant prophet in Islam, known for his righteousness and as the father of Yaqub (Jacob).

questions.q: Where did Ibrahim settle Isma'il and Hajirah by Allah's command?
opts: C. In the valley of Makkah (Bakkah) by the Sacred House
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ settled Isma'il and Hajirah in the valley of Makkah (Bakkah) near the Sacred House (Kaaba) by Allah's command to establish a community and a place of worship, which later became a central site for Islamic pilgrimage and faith.

questions.q: What did Allah command Ibrahim to do in a vision regarding his son?
questions.explanation: Allah commanded Prophet Ibrahim ﷺ in a vision to sacrifice his son as a test of faith and obedience. This event demonstrates Ibrahim's unwavering submission to Allah's will, and ultimately, Allah replaced his son with a ram, showing mercy and rewarding his devotion.

questions.q: Where did Allah place Adam and his wife after creating them?
opts: B. In a garden on earth
questions.explanation: After creating Adam and his wife, Allah placed them in Paradise (Jannah) as a place of peace and blessings, where they could live and enjoy His bounties before being sent to Earth. This is mentioned in the Quran to show the initial state of human existence.

questions.q: Which son was it that Ibrahim was commanded to sacrifice according to the most authentic Islamic view?
opts: D. It is disputed, but the majority hold it was Isma'il
questions.explanation: Ibrahim ﷺ was commanded to sacrifice his son Isma'il ﷺ according to the most authentic Islamic tradition, as mentioned in the Quran and Hadith. This event symbolizes complete submission to Allah's will and is commemorated annually during Eid al-Adha.

questions.q: What was the one thing Allah forbade Adam and Hawwa from doing in Paradise?
opts: A. Eating from a specific tree
questions.explanation: Allah forbade Adam and Hawwa from eating the fruit of a specific tree in Paradise as a test of their obedience and trust. This command was meant to teach them submission to Allah's will and the consequences of disobedience.

questions.q: How did Isma'il respond when his father told him about the command to sacrifice him?
opts: B. He said, "O my father, do as you are commanded; you will find me, if Allah wills, among the steadfast"
questions.explanation: Isma'il showed complete submission and trust in Allah by encouraging his father to fulfill the command, expressing his own readiness to be patient and steadfast in obedience to Allah’s will. This demonstrates the deep faith and obedience both had in fulfilling divine commands.

questions.q: What happened when Ibrahim attempted to sacrifice his son?
opts: B. Allah ransomed him with a great sacrifice (a ram from Paradise)
C. The knife would not cut
D. Both b and c - the knife would not cut, and then a ram was provided
questions.explanation: When Ibrahim ﷺ attempted to sacrifice his son as an act of obedience to Allah, the knife would not cut, signifying divine intervention, and then Allah provided a ram to be sacrificed instead, demonstrating mercy and the importance of submission to God's will.

questions.q: Where was Abu Dhar from?
opts: B. Ghifara (a tribe known for highway robbery)
questions.explanation: Abu Dhar was from the Ghifara tribe, which was known for its reputation in highway robbery. This background highlights his transformation after embracing Islam, showing how faith can change a person's character and actions.

questions.q: Why was the Prophet astonished when Abu Dhar embraced Islam?
opts: B. Because he was from Ghifara, a tribe with a notorious reputation
D. Because he was a leader
questions.explanation: The Prophet was astonished because Abu Dhar came from the Ghifara tribe, known for their strong opposition to Islam and rebellious nature, making his immediate acceptance of Islam surprising and significant.

questions.q: What was Abu Dhar's real name?
questions.explanation: Abu Dhar was a nickname meaning "Father of Dhar," while his real name was Jundub Ibn Janaadah. In Arab culture, it is common to use kunyas (nicknames) derived from a child’s name or characteristic as a form of respect and identity.

questions.q: What trick did Al-'Abbas use to rescue Abu Dhar?
opts: B. He told them Abu Dhar's tribe might rob their caravans
questions.explanation: Al-'Abbas warned the captors that Abu Dhar's tribe was known for attacking caravans, which made them fear potential retaliation. This clever tactic convinced them to release Abu Dhar to avoid conflict with his powerful tribe.

questions.q: What happened when Abu Dhar disgraced the idols of two women?
opts: B. They shouted, and men beat him until he fell unconscious
questions.explanation: When Abu Dhar disgraced the idols of two women, they shouted in anger, and the men protecting the idols beat him severely until he lost consciousness. This incident shows the strong attachment and defense people had for their idols before embracing Islam.

questions.q: What did the Prophet say when the whole tribes of Ghifar and Aslim came as Muslims?
opts: A. "May Allah forgive Ghifar" and "May Allah make peace with Aslim"
questions.explanation: When the tribes of Ghifar and Aslim embraced Islam, the Prophet ﷺ prayed for their forgiveness and peace, saying "May Allah forgive Ghifar" and "May Allah make peace with Aslim," highlighting his mercy and the importance of unity and reconciliation among new Muslim communities.

questions.q: What did the Prophet say about Abu Dhar's truthfulness?
opts: B. "The earth never carried above it, nor did the sky ever shade under it a more truthful tongue than Abu Dhar's"
questions.explanation: The Prophet ﷺ praised Abu Dhar's honesty by stating that no one on earth or under the sky spoke with greater truthfulness than him, highlighting Abu Dhar's exceptional integrity and sincerity in speech.

questions.q: What advice did the Prophet give Abu Dhar about dealing with oppressive commanders?
opts: B. "Be patient till you meet me"
questions.explanation: The Prophet advised Abu Dhar to be patient when dealing with oppressive commanders, emphasizing endurance and trust in divine justice rather than immediate confrontation, highlighting the importance of patience in facing injustice.

questions.q: What was Abu Dhar's famous chant when opposing wealth hoarders?
opts: B. "Announce to those who hoard up gold and silver, the warning of branding irons"
questions.explanation: Abu Dhar's chant warned that hoarding wealth without using it for good leads to severe punishment, symbolized by "branding irons," emphasizing Islam's teaching on the responsible use and circulation of wealth to help society and avoid greed.

questions.q: Which verse did Abu Dhar recite about hoarding gold and silver?
opts: B. 9:34-35 (On the Day when it shall be heated in the Fire of Hell, and with it their foreheads... shall be branded)
questions.explanation: Surah At-Tawbah (9:34-35) warns against hoarding gold and silver without spending it in Allah’s cause, describing how these treasures will be heated in Hellfire and branded on the hoarders’ foreheads as a punishment for their greed and neglect of charity.

questions.q: Where did Abu Dhar go to oppose the abuse of power and wealth?
questions.explanation: Abu Dhar went to Syria to oppose the abuse of power and wealth under Mu'aawiyah's rule because he was known for his strong stance against injustice and corruption, emphasizing the Prophet’s teachings on social justice and equality.

questions.q: What did Mu'aawiyah write to Caliph 'Uthmaan about Abu Dhar?
opts: B. "Abu Dhar spoils the people in Syria"
questions.explanation: Mu'aawiyah wrote to Caliph 'Uthmaan expressing concern that Abu Dhar's outspoken criticism was causing unrest among the people in Syria, as Abu Dhar was known for his strict views on wealth and social justice, which challenged the established order.

questions.q: Where did Abu Dhar choose to go after his disagreement with 'Uthmaan?
questions.explanation: After his disagreement with 'Uthmaan, Abu Dhar chose to go to Ar-Rabadhah, a remote desert area, to live a simple and ascetic life away from political conflicts, reflecting his commitment to justice and detachment from worldly power.

questions.q: How did Abu Dhar respond to a delegation from Kufa asking him to lead a revolution?
opts: B. He said he would patiently obey even if crucified, for Allah's reward
questions.explanation: Abu Dhar emphasized patience and obedience to Allah's will rather than rebellion, expressing willingness to endure severe hardship like crucifixion for divine reward. This reflects his commitment to spiritual principles over political power.

questions.q: What seven things did the Prophet advise Abu Dhar to do?
opts: A. Love the poor, look to inferiors, never ask anyone for anything, be kind to relatives, say the truth, never fear a critic's censure, frequently say "There is no power nor might except Allah's"
B. Fight, earn wealth, be proud, ignore others
C. Pray, fast, give charity, perform Hajj
questions.explanation: The Prophet ﷺ advised Abu Dhar to embody humility and sincerity by loving the poor, respecting those beneath him, avoiding asking others for help, being kind to relatives, speaking the truth, not fearing criticism, and frequently affirming reliance on Allah’s power, emphasizing strong faith and ethical conduct in daily life.

questions.q: What did 'Aliy say about Abu Dhar?
opts: B. "There is no one nowadays who is nonchalant about people's criticism - as far as Allah and His rules are concerned - except Abu Dhar"
questions.explanation: 'Aliy praised Abu Dhar for his steadfastness and indifference to people's criticism when it came to upholding Allah's commands, highlighting Abu Dhar's sincere commitment to faith over public opinion.

questions.q: How did Abu Dhar die?
opts: B. Alone in the desert of Ar-Rabadhah
questions.explanation: Abu Dhar died alone in the desert of Ar-Rabadhah because he chose to live a simple, ascetic life away from society, reflecting his commitment to piety and detachment from worldly matters. This solitude emphasized his dedication to spiritual purity and devotion to Allah.

questions.q: Who led the caravan that found Abu Dhar's body?
questions.explanation: ʿAbd Allāh ibn Masʿūd led the caravan that discovered Abu Dharr's body because he was a close companion of the Prophet Muhammad and trusted to handle important matters, including verifying the fate of fellow companions during turbulent times.

questions.q: What prophecy did the Prophet make about Abu Dhar during the Tabuk expedition?
opts: B. "He walks alone, dies alone, and resurrects alone"
questions.explanation: The Prophet Muhammad foretold that Abu Dhar would live a solitary life, face hardships alone, and be resurrected alone, highlighting his unique dedication and isolation due to his outspoken nature and uncompromising stance on justice. This prophecy reflects Abu Dhar's distinct character among the companions.

questions.q: At what age did 'Abd Allah Ibn 'Umar first accompany his father to the Prophet?
questions.explanation: 'Abd Allah Ibn 'Umar first accompanied his father, Ibn 'Umar, to the Prophet Muhammad at the age of 13, allowing him to learn directly from the Prophet's teachings and practices during his formative years. This early exposure helped him become a prominent narrator of Hadith.

questions.q: What did Ibn 'Umar do that provoked 'Aa'ishah to say, "No one followed the Prophet's steps in his coming and going as Ibn 'Umar did"?
opts: B. He imitated the Prophet in every detail, even where the Prophet's camel turned
questions.explanation: Ibn 'Umar closely followed the Prophet ﷺ in all his actions, including mimicking the exact movements of the Prophet's camel, showing his deep love and dedication. This meticulous imitation impressed 'Aa'ishah, highlighting Ibn 'Umar's commitment to following the Prophet's example precisely.

questions.q: To which city was Prophet Lut sent?
questions.explanation: Prophet Lut ﷺ was sent to the city of Sodom, which was known for its immoral behavior. His mission was to call the people to repentance and warn them against their sinful actions.

questions.q: What was the major sin of the people of Sodom?
opts: B. Men having sex with men instead of with women
questions.explanation: The major sin of the people of Sodom was engaging in homosexual acts, which violated the natural order of relationships prescribed in Islam. Their persistent immoral behavior and rejection of Prophet Lut's ﷺ message led to their destruction as a divine punishment.

questions.q: Who was the only member of Lut's family who did not believe and was destroyed?
questions.explanation: Lut's wife did not believe in his message and disobeyed the command to leave the city, so she was destroyed along with the disbelievers. This shows that faith and obedience are essential, regardless of family ties.

questions.q: How did the angels appear to Lut?
questions.explanation: The angels appeared to Prophet Lut ﷺ as handsome young men to test the people of Sodom and Gomorrah, who were known for their immoral behavior, and to protect Lut ﷺ from harm while delivering their divine message.

questions.q: What punishment did Allah send upon the people of Sodom?
opts: B. The town was turned upside down and rained with stones of baked clay
questions.explanation: Allah punished the people of Sodom by overturning their town and sending stones of baked clay upon them because of their persistent sinful behavior and rejection of Prophet Lut's ﷺ message. This severe punishment served as a warning against immorality and disobedience to Allah.

questions.q: Along with which prophets is Dhul-Kifl mentioned in the Quran?
questions.explanation: Dhul-Kifl is mentioned in the Quran alongside prophets Ishmael and Idris as examples of patient and righteous individuals. This grouping highlights their steadfastness and dedication to God's guidance, emphasizing their exemplary character among the prophets.

questions.q: What does the name "Dhul-Kifl" literally mean?
opts: B. Possessor of, or giving, a double requital or portion
questions.explanation: "Dhul-Kifl" literally means "possessor of, or giving, a double portion or requital," indicating someone entrusted with a double responsibility or reward. This reflects the Prophet's role in upholding justice and fulfilling duties with great care and fairness.

questions.q: What did Allah inform the angels about before creating Adam?
opts: A. He was going to create angels in heaven
B. He was going to place mankind generations after generations on earth
C. He was going to create jinn from fire
D. He was going to destroy the earth
questions.explanation: Before creating Adam, Allah informed the angels that He would place successive generations of mankind on earth to live and fulfill His divine plan, highlighting humanity's special role and responsibility in creation.

questions.q: What did Allah teach Adam that He did not teach the angels?
questions.explanation: Allah taught Adam the names of everything to demonstrate his unique knowledge and ability to learn, which distinguished him from the angels who did not possess this specific knowledge. This teaching highlighted human potential and wisdom granted by Allah.

questions.q: What happened when Adam and Eve ate from the forbidden tree?
opts: B. Their private parts became manifest and they began covering themselves with leaves
C. They were immediately sent to hell
D. They forgot who they were
questions.explanation: When Adam and Eve ate from the forbidden tree, their innocence was lost, and they became aware of their nakedness, causing their private parts to become visible. This led them to cover themselves with leaves as a sign of newfound shame and awareness.

questions.q: Who committed the first murder among Adam's children?
questions.explanation: Cain (Qabil) committed the first murder by killing his brother Abel (Habil) out of jealousy, as mentioned in Islamic teachings. This event marks the first instance of murder among humanity after Prophet Adam ﷺ.

questions.q: What did Allah send to show Cain how to bury his brother?
opts: B. A raven that scratched the ground
C. A vision in a dream
questions.explanation: Allah sent a raven that scratched the ground to demonstrate to Cain how to bury his brother, Abel, teaching him the proper way to cover the body after the first murder in human history. This act served as a lesson in respect for the deceased and the importance of burial.

questions.q: How many psalms (scriptures) were sent down to Seth according to Abu Dhar's narration?
questions.explanation: According to Abu Dhar's narration, 50 psalms were revealed to Seth ﷺ, highlighting the continuation of divine guidance through successive prophets. This emphasizes that scriptures were sent to various prophets before the Quran, each tailored to their communities.

questions.q: According to the hadith, who argued with Adam about his sin that caused mankind to be expelled from Paradise?
questions.explanation: Moses argued with Adam about his sin in a hadith, highlighting human fallibility and the consequences of disobedience. This dialogue emphasizes the importance of repentance and God's mercy after Adam's mistake in Paradise.

questions.q: How is Prophet Idris described in the Quran?
opts: B. A man of truth and a prophet
questions.explanation: Prophet Idris ﷺ is described in the Quran as a man of truth and a prophet, highlighting his honesty and his role as a messenger of Allah. This emphasizes his exemplary character and his responsibility to convey divine guidance.

questions.q: Where was Prophet Idris born and raised?
questions.explanation: Prophet Idris ﷺ is traditionally believed to have been born and raised in Babylon, an ancient city known for its early civilization. This location is significant as it connects him to early human history and the spread of knowledge and wisdom.

questions.q: What was Prophet Idris the first to invent according to the text?
opts: B. The basic form of writing
questions.explanation: Prophet Idris ﷺ is recognized as the first to invent the basic form of writing, which allowed knowledge to be recorded and transmitted across generations. This invention played a crucial role in the development of human civilization and communication.

questions.q: Which of the following is a wise saying attributed to Prophet Idris?
opts: A. "Money is the root of all evil"
B. "Happy is he who looks at his own deeds and appoints them as pleaders to his Lord"
questions.explanation: This saying emphasizes self-accountability, encouraging individuals to reflect on their actions and let them advocate on their behalf before God, highlighting Prophet Idris's wisdom in promoting personal responsibility and spiritual mindfulness.

questions.q: Where did Prophet Idris and his followers go when most people turned away from his message?
questions.explanation: Prophet Idris ﷺ and his followers migrated to Egypt because many people rejected his message, seeking a place where they could practice their faith peacefully and continue spreading guidance. Egypt became a safe refuge for them during that difficult time.

questions.q: How long did Prophet Nuh stay among his people inviting them to Allah?
questions.explanation: Prophet Nuh ﷺ preached to his people for 950 years, patiently inviting them to worship Allah alone and abandon idolatry. This long period highlights his perseverance and dedication despite their persistent rejection.

questions.q: What were the names of the idols worshipped by Nuh's people?
opts: B. Wadd, Suwa, Yaghuth, Ya'uq, Nasr
questions.explanation: Nuh's people worshipped five main idols named Wadd, Suwa, Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr, as mentioned in the Quran (Surah Nuh 71:23). These idols represented false deities that Nuh ﷺ warned his people against, emphasizing monotheism and the worship of Allah alone.

questions.q: What sign did Allah give Nuh that the flood was about to begin?
opts: B. The oven at Nuh's house gushing forth water
questions.explanation: Allah gave Nuh ﷺ the sign of the oven in his house gushing forth water, indicating the imminent flood. This unusual event symbolized the beginning of the great deluge that would cover the earth as a divine punishment.

questions.q: How many believers were with Nuh on the ark according to different scholars?
questions.explanation: Scholars differ on the number of believers with Nuh ﷺ on the ark, estimating between 10 and 80 based on various narrations and interpretations. This range reflects the small group who accepted his message amid widespread rejection.

questions.q: What happened to Nuh's son who did not board the ark?
opts: B. He was among the drowned
D. He climbed a mountain and survived
questions.explanation: Nuh's son chose not to join him on the ark and sought refuge on a mountain, but the floodwaters overwhelmed him. This story highlights the importance of obeying Allah's commands and the consequences of disbelief.

questions.q: According to the hadith, what two things did Nuh advise his sons about on his deathbed?
opts: A. To be wealthy and powerful
B. To believe in Allah's oneness and to avoid pride and associating partners with Allah
C. To build cities and conquer nations
D. To follow their ancestors' religion
questions.explanation: Nuh ﷺ advised his sons to firmly believe in the oneness of Allah, emphasizing monotheism, and warned them against pride and associating partners with Allah, as these lead to shirk, which is the gravest sin in Islam. This guidance highlights the core message of all prophets.

questions.q: What was the name of the Pharaoh during the time of Musa's birth according to some narrations?
opts: The text does not specify a name, referring to him only as "Pharaoh"
questions.explanation: The Quran and authentic Islamic texts do not mention the Pharaoh's specific name during Musa's birth, referring to him simply as "Pharaoh" to emphasize his role and tyranny rather than his personal identity. This highlights the focus on his actions rather than his individual identity.

questions.q: What dream did Pharaoh have that prompted him to take action against the Children of Israel?
opts: He dreamed of a great flood destroying Egypt
He saw a fire coming from Jerusalem that burned all the Copts but left the Israelites
He dreamed he was drowning in the sea
He dreamed that his crown fell off his head
questions.explanation: Pharaoh's dream symbolized a fire destroying the Copts (Egyptians) but sparing the Israelites, indicating the rising power and protection of the Children of Israel. This vision alarmed Pharaoh, prompting him to oppress them to prevent their growing influence and potential threat to his rule.

questions.q: Who interpreted Pharaoh's dream?
questions.explanation: Pharaoh's dream was interpreted by his priests and soothsayers, who were skilled in dream interpretation and divination in ancient Egypt. Their role was to provide explanations and predictions based on the dreams, which Pharaoh valued before Prophet Musa (AS) offered a true interpretation from Allah.

questions.q: What was the interpretation given to Pharaoh?
opts: A great famine would strike Egypt
A boy would be born among the Israelites who would overthrow his kingdom
He would die within the year
questions.explanation: Pharaoh was warned through a dream that a boy from the Israelites would be born who would grow strong and eventually challenge and overthrow his rule, symbolizing the coming of Prophet Musa (Moses) who would lead the Israelites to freedom.

questions.q: What decree did Pharaoh issue after this interpretation?
opts: To kill every newborn male among the Israelites
To exile the Israelites from Egypt
To increase taxes on the Israelites
questions.explanation: Pharaoh ordered the killing of every newborn male among the Israelites to prevent the rise of a future leader who might challenge his authority, as the interpretation of the dream suggested the birth of a boy who would overthrow him. This decree aimed to suppress the Israelite population and maintain his power.

questions.q: According to the detailed narration from Ubayy ibn Ka'b, what was Pharaoh's policy regarding the killing of Israelite boys?
opts: He killed all boys every year
He killed boys one year and spared them the next, alternating
He killed all boys for ten years straight
questions.explanation: Pharaoh's policy was to kill Israelite boys born in one year and spare those born the next, alternating annually. This cruel strategy aimed to control the population while maintaining some labor force, as narrated by Ubayy ibn Ka'b in Islamic tradition.

questions.q: What was the reasoning behind this alternating policy?
opts: To completely wipe out the Israelites
To prevent the Israelite population from being completely wiped out while still preventing the prophesied boy from being born
To give the Israelites false hope
questions.explanation: The alternating policy ensured that not all Israelite male infants were killed, preserving the community's survival, while still fulfilling the decree that a prophesied boy would be born to challenge Pharaoh’s tyranny. This balance protected the future leader without allowing total annihilation.

questions.q: How many Israelite boys were being killed each year according to some reports?
opts: The exact number is not specified
questions.explanation: Some reports mention that Pharaoh ordered the killing of Israelite boys, but the exact number is not specified in the Quran or authentic sources, emphasizing the severity of the oppression rather than a precise figure.

questions.q: What was the reaction of the Israelites to this decree?
opts: They were terrified and prayed to Allah for deliverance
questions.explanation: The Israelites were terrified by the decree because it threatened their lives and well-being, leading them to sincerely pray to Allah for protection and deliverance from their distress. This shows their reliance on divine help in times of hardship.

questions.q: How long did this policy continue before Musa's birth?
opts: The text does not specify exactly how long it had been in place
questions.explanation: The Quran and authentic sources do not provide a specific duration for how long the policy of killing newborn Israelite boys was in place before Musa's birth. This lack of detail emphasizes the focus on the event's significance rather than its exact timeline.

questions.q: What did Pharaoh's advisors tell him about the Israelite population under this policy?
opts: That it was growing despite the killings
That the Israelites would soon be extinct
That the older generation was dying and the younger generation was being killed, so they would have to do all the labor themselves
That the policy was working perfectly
questions.explanation: Pharaoh's advisors explained that because the older Israelites were dying naturally and the younger ones were being killed by Pharaoh's decree, the population was declining, which meant fewer workers were available to do the labor. This highlighted the unsustainable nature of Pharaoh's harsh policy against the Israelites.

questions.q: What advice did the advisors give Pharaoh to address this labor problem?
opts: To kill boys one year and spare them the next, so the older generation would be replaced by the spared boys
To enslave the Israelites instead of killing them
To bring in slaves from other lands
questions.explanation: Pharaoh's advisors suggested killing all newborn boys every other year to control the population and labor force, aiming to weaken the Israelites by reducing their numbers while maintaining a workforce from the spared boys. This harsh policy was intended to prevent the Israelites from becoming too powerful.

questions.q: Which year was it when Musa's mother became pregnant with him?
questions.explanation: Musa's mother became pregnant during a "sparing year," a time when Pharaoh's decree to kill newborn Israelite boys was temporarily relaxed, allowing her to safely carry and give birth to Musa (AS). This period was crucial for his survival and future mission.

questions.q: What was the name of the chief advisor or minister of Pharaoh mentioned in the Quran and discussed by Ibn Kathir?
questions.explanation: Haman was the chief minister or advisor to Pharaoh mentioned in the Quran, known for his opposition to Prophet Musa (AS). Ibn Kathir explains that Haman played a significant role in supporting Pharaoh's tyranny and rejecting Musa's message.

questions.q: According to Ibn Kathir, what was the role of Haman in Pharaoh's regime?
opts: He was the builder and minister in charge of construction projects
He was the commander of the army
questions.explanation: Ibn Kathir explains that Haman was a high-ranking official under Pharaoh, responsible for overseeing construction projects, including building towers and fortifications. His role was to support Pharaoh's regime by organizing labor and resources for these large-scale works.

questions.q: What was the name of Musa's mother?
questions.explanation: Musa's mother is known as Yukabad (or Yuhanidh) in Islamic tradition. She showed great faith by trusting Allah's plan when she placed baby Musa in a basket to save him from Pharaoh's decree.

questions.q: What was the name of Musa's father?
opts: The text does not specify his name clearly
questions.explanation: Musa's (Moses) father was named Imran, as mentioned in Islamic tradition and the Quran. This name is significant because it links Musa to a noble lineage, emphasizing his prophetic heritage.

questions.q: How did Musa's mother feel when she became pregnant with him, knowing it was a sparing year?
opts: Extremely anxious and fearful despite it being a sparing year
questions.explanation: Musa's mother felt extremely anxious and fearful because the Pharaoh had ordered the killing of newborn Israelite boys, making it a dangerous time despite the year being designated as sparing. Her fear was for Musa’s safety in a hostile environment.

questions.q: What did Allah inspire (awha) to Musa's mother to do when she gave birth?
opts: To hide him in a cave
To suckle him, and when she feared for him, to put him in a chest and cast him into the river
To take him to the palace herself
To give him to a nurse
questions.explanation: Allah inspired Musa's mother to breastfeed him to ensure his survival and, when she feared for his safety due to Pharaoh's oppression, to place him in a chest and set it afloat on the river, trusting Allah's protection and plan for Musa's future.

questions.q: What kind of revelation (wahi) was this to Musa's mother?
opts: A revelation of inspiration and guidance (ilham wa irshad), like that given to the bee
A vision she had while awake
questions.explanation: Musa's mother received a subtle form of revelation called ilham wa irshad, which means inspiration and guidance from Allah, rather than direct speech or vision. This type of wahi is similar to the inspiration given to the bee, guiding actions without explicit commands.

questions.q: What reassurance did Allah give Musa's mother along with this command?
opts: That she would have another son
That she should not fear or grieve, and that Allah would return the child to her and make him a messenger
That Pharaoh would adopt the child
That the child would die peacefully
questions.explanation: Allah reassured Musa's mother by telling her not to fear or grieve because He would protect her son, return him to her safely, and appoint him as a messenger to guide the people. This comfort emphasized Allah’s control and care over the situation.

questions.q: What did Musa's mother do after giving birth to Musa?
opts: She kept him at home secretly
She made a chest, placed him in it, and cast it into the Nile River
She gave him to a neighbor
She took him to the desert
questions.explanation: Musa's mother placed him in a chest and set it afloat on the Nile to protect him from Pharaoh's decree to kill newborn Israelite boys, trusting in Allah's plan for his safety and future. This act demonstrated her faith and courage in a dangerous time.

questions.q: What was the chest made of according to some narrations?
questions.explanation: According to some narrations, the chest (ark) was made of papyrus reeds waterproofed with pitch to ensure it was light and buoyant, protecting baby Musa (AS) from water while floating on the Nile. This material choice highlights the practical and miraculous nature of his preservation.

questions.q: Where did the river carry the chest?
opts: To the gardens of Pharaoh's palace
It got stuck in the reeds
questions.explanation: The river carried the chest containing baby Musa (AS) to the gardens of Pharaoh's palace, where he was found and raised. This event ensured his safety from Pharaoh's decree to kill newborn Israelite boys.

questions.q: Who found the chest in the river?
opts: The servants or maidservants of Pharaoh's wife, Asiyah
questions.explanation: The chest containing baby Musa (AS) was discovered by the servants or maidservants of Pharaoh's wife, Asiyah, who then brought him to her. This event is significant as it led to Musa being raised in Pharaoh's palace despite the decree to kill Israelite male infants.

questions.q: What did the servants think was in the chest?
questions.explanation: The servants thought the chest contained treasure because it was unusual and valuable-looking, which made them curious. However, it actually held baby Musa (Moses) to protect him from harm, as part of Allah’s plan to save him.

questions.q: When they opened the chest and saw the baby, what happened?
opts: Allah cast love for the baby into their hearts, especially into the heart of Asiyah
They were afraid and wanted to return him to the river
questions.explanation: When the chest containing baby Musa (AS) was opened, Allah placed love and compassion for him into the hearts of those present, especially Asiyah, the wife of Pharaoh, which protected and nurtured him despite the difficult circumstances. This divine love ensured his safety and care.

questions.q: Who was Asiyah?
questions.explanation: Asiyah was the wife of Pharaoh who, despite her husband's tyranny, believed in Allah and supported Prophet Musa (AS). She is honored in Islam for her faith and patience under oppression.

questions.q: What did Asiyah say to Pharaoh when she wanted to keep the baby?
opts: "He will be a comfort to me and to you; do not kill him"
"He is a gift from the gods"
"We can use him as a hostage"
questions.explanation: Asiyah, Pharaoh's wife, pleaded for the baby Musa (AS) to be spared, expressing that he would bring comfort to both her and Pharaoh, showing her compassion and faith despite Pharaoh's harsh decree to kill male infants.

questions.q: What was Pharaoh's response to Asiyah's request?
opts: He said "He will be a comfort to you, but not to me"
He ordered the baby killed immediately
questions.explanation: Pharaoh acknowledged that Musa (AS) would bring comfort to Asiyah, his wife, but rejected him as a threat to his own power and authority, showing his harsh and oppressive nature. This highlights Pharaoh's cruelty despite Asiyah's compassion.

questions.q: According to a hadith mentioned by Ibn Kathir, what did the Prophet ﷺ say about Pharaoh's response?
opts: "Pharaoh was right not to love him"
"If Pharaoh had said 'a comfort to me as well,' Allah would have guided him as He guided Asiyah"
questions.explanation: This hadith highlights that Pharaoh's rejection was due to his arrogance; had he sincerely sought guidance like Asiyah, his wife, Allah would have guided him. It emphasizes the importance of humility and openness to faith for receiving divine guidance.

questions.q: What does the phrase "qurratu `aynin" (comfort of the eye) mean?
opts: One whose sight brings joy and comfort
questions.explanation: The phrase "qurratu `aynin" means someone whose presence or sight brings great joy, peace, and comfort to the heart, much like a refreshing and soothing experience for the eyes. It highlights deep emotional and spiritual happiness.

questions.q: What was the state of Musa's mother's heart after she cast him into the river?
opts: Her heart became "empty" - she could think of nothing but Musa, and almost disclosed his matter
questions.explanation: Musa's mother felt her heart become "empty" because her love and concern for him overwhelmed her thoughts, making it difficult to focus on anything else. This intense emotion nearly led her to reveal the secret of Musa's safety, showing the deep bond between mother and child.

questions.q: What did Allah say about the state of Musa's mother's heart?
opts: "And the heart of Musa's mother became empty [of all else]"
questions.explanation: Allah described Musa's mother's heart as becoming empty, meaning she was fully devoted and focused on trusting Allah's plan for her son, showing her deep faith and surrender during a difficult time. This highlights her spiritual strength and reliance on Allah.

questions.q: How close did she come to revealing the truth about Musa?
opts: She almost disclosed his matter if Allah had not bound fast her heart
questions.explanation: She nearly revealed the truth about Musa (AS), but Allah restrained her heart to prevent this, protecting Musa's mission and ensuring divine plans were not disrupted. This demonstrates Allah’s control over human intentions and outcomes.

questions.q: What did Musa's mother tell his sister to do?
opts: "Follow him and see what happens to him"
"Go to the palace and demand him back"
questions.explanation: Musa's mother instructed his sister to follow him secretly after placing him in the river, so she could observe what would happen to him and be ready to assist if needed. This ensured Musa's safety and allowed her to reunite him with their family later.

questions.q: How did Musa's sister follow the chest without being noticed?
opts: She walked right behind the servants
She watched from a distance while pretending to be unrelated
She disguised herself as a servant
questions.explanation: Musa's sister followed the chest from a distance, carefully hiding her identity to avoid drawing attention. By pretending to be unrelated, she ensured she could protect her brother without risking detection by others.

questions.q: What problem arose regarding Musa in the palace?
opts: He refused to suckle from any wet nurse
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) refused to suckle from any wet nurse because Allah had protected him with a special blessing, indicating his unique status and divine protection from infancy, which was unusual and caused concern in the palace.

questions.q: How did the women of the palace try to feed Musa?
opts: They brought many wet nurses, but he refused them all
They fed him with a bottle
questions.explanation: The women of the palace brought many wet nurses to feed Musa (AS), but he refused them all because Allah had appointed his own mother to be his nurse, reuniting them and fulfilling divine wisdom in his upbringing.

questions.q: Why did Musa refuse all the wet nurses according to the divine plan?
opts: Because he was a difficult baby
Because Allah had forbidden him from accepting any nurse other than his mother
Because he didn't like Egyptian women
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) refused all wet nurses because Allah had commanded that only his biological mother should nurse him, ensuring her protection and fulfilling the divine plan for his upbringing under her care. This was a special decree to safeguard Musa’s early life.

questions.q: What did Musa's sister do when she saw they were desperate to find a wet nurse?
opts: She said, "Shall I direct you to a household that will be responsible for him for you while they are to him sincere?"
She ran back to tell her mother
She offered to nurse him herself
questions.explanation: Musa's sister offered to help by suggesting a trustworthy family to care for baby Musa, showing her concern and wisdom in ensuring his safety and well-being during a difficult time. This highlights her protective role and quick thinking in the story.

questions.q: How did the palace officials respond to her offer?
opts: They told her to bring the woman immediately
questions.explanation: The palace officials responded promptly to her offer because they needed to verify the situation quickly, demonstrating the importance of immediate action in matters concerning the safety and well-being of individuals in the royal household. This reflects the seriousness with which such offers were treated in that context.

questions.q: When Musa's mother came and nursed him, what happened?
questions.explanation: When Musa's mother came to nurse him, he accepted her immediately, showing a natural recognition and bond despite the time apart. This event highlights the special connection and divine protection granted to Musa (AS) from infancy.

questions.q: How did the palace officials react to this?
opts: They were amazed and asked her about herself
questions.explanation: The palace officials were amazed by her appearance and demeanor, prompting them to inquire about her identity and background. This reaction shows their curiosity and respect toward someone who stood out in the royal environment.

questions.q: What did Musa's mother say when asked about herself?
opts: She revealed she was his mother
She said she was a woman with sweet milk and a gentle nature
questions.explanation: Musa's mother described herself as a woman with sweet milk and a gentle nature to emphasize her kindness and nurturing qualities, highlighting her compassionate character when questioned about herself. This reflects her humility and the loving care she provided to Musa (AS).

questions.q: What was the outcome of this?
opts: They kept Musa in the palace with his mother as his nurse
They sent him home with his mother permanently
questions.explanation: Pharaoh’s wife insisted on keeping Musa (AS) in the palace and allowed his mother to nurse him, ensuring his safety and care despite Pharaoh’s initial decree to harm the Israelite infants. This act preserved Musa’s life and allowed him to grow up with knowledge of both his people and the palace.

questions.q: What did Allah say about this in the Quran?
opts: "So We restored him to his mother that she might be content and not grieve and that she would know that the promise of Allah is true"
"And We returned him to his mother by chance"
questions.explanation: Allah mentions in the Quran that He returned Prophet Musa (AS) to his mother to comfort her and confirm the truth of His promise, showing His mercy and the fulfillment of His divine plan. This highlights Allah’s care and the certainty of His promises.

questions.q: How long did Musa remain with his mother as his nurse?
opts: The text does not specify the exact duration
questions.explanation: The Quran and authentic sources mention that Musa's mother was appointed as his nurse by Allah's command, but they do not specify the exact length of time she cared for him. The focus is on the trust and divine protection given to Musa during his early life.

questions.q: What did Allah later remind Musa of about this period?
opts: "And I bestowed upon you love from Me that you would be brought up under My eye"
"You were fortunate to be in the palace"
questions.explanation: Allah reminded Musa that during his early life, He granted him special love and care, raising him under His direct protection and guidance. This highlights the divine support and nurturing Musa received, preparing him for his prophetic mission.

questions.q: What does "under My eye" (ala `ayni) mean in this context?
opts: Allah's protection, care, and nurturing were upon him
He was in Allah's sight literally
questions.explanation: "Under My eye" (ala `ayni) signifies that Allah was closely watching over, protecting, and nurturing Musa (AS) with special care and attention, ensuring his safety and well-being throughout his life.

questions.q: How was Musa treated in Pharaoh's palace?
opts: He was treated as a slave
He was raised as a prince, with the best food, clothing, and upbringing
He was kept in a separate room
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) was brought up in Pharaoh's palace as a prince, receiving excellent care, education, and luxury. This special treatment was part of God's plan to prepare him for his future role as a prophet and leader of the Israelites.

questions.q: What was the purpose of this luxurious upbringing in the divine plan?
opts: To make Musa soft and weak
To prepare him for leadership with the best education and manners
To make him forget his people
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) was raised in a royal environment to equip him with knowledge, skills, and refined character, enabling him to effectively lead and guide his people. This upbringing ensured he could confront Pharaoh and fulfill his prophetic mission with confidence and wisdom.

questions.q: What did the commentators say about the phrase "and I bestowed upon you love from Me"?
opts: That everyone who saw Musa loved him
questions.explanation: The phrase "and I bestowed upon you love from Me" is interpreted by commentators to mean both Allah’s special love for Prophet Musa (AS) and the love that others feel towards him due to Allah’s blessing, highlighting the unique spiritual bond and respect granted to him.

questions.q: What incident happened when Musa was young that affected his speech?
opts: He fell and hurt his tongue
He was burned by hot coal
He had a speech impediment from birth
He was struck by an angel
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) was young, he accidentally touched hot coal, which caused a burn on his tongue. This incident affected his speech, making him somewhat hesitant or slow in speaking, as mentioned in the Quran.

questions.q: What was the test given to young Musa when he pulled Pharaoh's beard?
opts: He was given a choice between a jewel and a hot coal
He was asked why he did it
questions.explanation: When young Musa (AS) pulled Pharaoh's beard, he was tested by being offered a choice between a jewel and a hot coal to see his reaction; he wisely chose the hot coal, showing patience and restraint rather than greed or anger.

questions.q: What was the wisdom behind this test?
opts: To prove his innocence (since he chose the coal, it showed he didn't understand the action of pulling the beard)
questions.explanation: The test was designed to demonstrate Prophet Musa's (AS) innocence by showing he did not grasp the intention behind pulling the beard, as he chose the coal instead. This indicated he was unaware of the wrongful act, proving he did not commit the offense.

questions.q: What was the lasting effect of this incident?
opts: He developed a speech impediment (a knot in his tongue)
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) spoke to Pharaoh, he experienced a speech impediment, which became a lasting effect. This challenge highlighted his reliance on his brother Harun (AS) to assist in communication, demonstrating the importance of patience and support in fulfilling Allah’s mission.

questions.q: How old was Musa when he left the palace?
opts: He left as a young child
The text does not specify exactly when he left
questions.explanation: The exact age of Musa (AS) when he left the palace is not specified in Islamic texts, as the focus is more on his mission and experiences rather than precise timelines. This highlights the importance of his prophetic role over specific biographical details.

questions.q: Did Musa know he was an Israelite while growing up in the palace?
opts: No, he thought he was Egyptian
Yes, his mother likely told him when he was older
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) was raised in Pharaoh’s palace but was aware of his Israelite heritage, as his mother likely informed him when he grew older. This knowledge helped him understand his identity and mission to guide the Israelites.

questions.q: Why did Musa go out into the city one day?
opts: During a time when people were resting, and he saw two men fighting
questions.explanation: Musa went out into the city during a time when people were resting to find peace and quiet. While there, he saw two men fighting, which led him to intervene and ultimately marked a significant moment in his life and mission.

questions.q: Who were the two men fighting?
questions.explanation: The two men fighting were an Israelite and an Egyptian, as mentioned in the story of Prophet Musa (AS). This incident led Musa to intervene and defend the Israelite, which eventually caused significant consequences in his life and mission.

questions.q: What did the Israelite ask Musa to do?
opts: To help him against the Egyptian
questions.explanation: The Israelites asked Musa (AS) to help them against the oppression and cruelty of the Egyptians, seeking deliverance from their harsh slavery and injustice under Pharaoh's rule. Musa was chosen by Allah to lead and free them.

questions.q: How did Musa intervene?
opts: He struck the Egyptian with his fist
He shouted at them to stop
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) intervened when he saw an Egyptian oppressing an Israelite by striking the Egyptian with his fist to stop the injustice. This act was a response to protect the oppressed and uphold justice.

questions.q: What was the result of Musa's strike?
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) struck, he unintentionally killed an Egyptian who was oppressing a fellow Israelite. This event led Musa to flee Egypt out of fear of punishment, marking a significant turning point in his life and mission.

questions.q: What was Musa's immediate reaction?
opts: He realized this was from the work of Satan and sought Allah's forgiveness
He told the Israelite to leave
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) saw the magicians' tricks, he recognized them as illusions inspired by Satan, not true miracles. Understanding this, he immediately turned to Allah in repentance and sought His forgiveness, showing his strong faith and awareness of divine guidance.

questions.q: What did Musa pray to Allah after this incident?
opts: "My Lord, I have wronged myself, so forgive me"
"My Lord, protect me from the wrongdoers"
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) prayed, "My Lord, I have wronged myself, so forgive me," showing his humility and seeking Allah’s mercy after unintentionally harming someone. This prayer highlights the importance of recognizing one's mistakes and turning to Allah for forgiveness.

questions.q: Did Allah forgive Musa?
opts: He told him to repent more
questions.explanation: Allah forgave Musa (AS) after he sincerely repented for his mistakes, demonstrating Allah’s mercy and willingness to forgive His prophets when they turn back to Him with humility and sincerity. This teaches the importance of repentance and Allah’s compassion.

questions.q: What did Musa do the next day in the city?
opts: He went out again, fearful and vigilant
questions.explanation: Musa went out the next day feeling fearful and vigilant because he was cautious about encountering the same hostility he faced previously. This shows his awareness and concern for his safety while trying to fulfill his responsibilities.

questions.q: Who did Musa see fighting the next day?
opts: The same Israelite and another Egyptian
The same Israelite and a different Egyptian
questions.explanation: Musa saw the same Israelite he had defended the previous day fighting with an Egyptian, which led him to intervene again. This event marked the beginning of his mission to free the Israelites from oppression in Egypt.

questions.q: What did the Israelite say to Musa when he saw him?
opts: "Are you going to kill me as you killed the man yesterday?"
questions.explanation: The Israelite said this because Musa had previously killed an Egyptian who was oppressing the Israelites, so they feared he might harm them as well. This reflects their misunderstanding of Musa’s intentions and the tense situation before his prophethood was fully established.

questions.q: How did the Egyptian learn about the previous day's killing?
questions.explanation: The Egyptian learned about the previous day's killing because an Israelite accidentally revealed the incident while speaking, unintentionally exposing what had happened. This slip led to the Egyptian becoming aware of the event.

questions.q: What happened after the Israelite's words?
opts: The Egyptian ran to inform Pharaoh's authorities
questions.explanation: After the Israelites spoke about their situation, an Egyptian quickly ran to inform Pharaoh's authorities, alerting them to the growing unrest and the potential threat posed by the Israelites, which led to Pharaoh's increased oppression of them.

questions.q: Who came to warn Musa?
opts: A man from the farthest part of the city running, saying "O Musa, the chiefs are conspiring against you to kill you, so leave"
questions.explanation: A man from the farthest part of the city warned Musa (AS) about the chiefs' plot to kill him, urging him to leave immediately. This warning helped Musa avoid danger and continue his mission safely.

questions.q: Who was this man who warned Musa?
opts: He was an angel in human form
The text identifies him as the believing man of Pharaoh's family
questions.explanation: The man who warned Musa (AS) is described as a believing member of Pharaoh's family, showing that faith can exist even within an oppressive environment. His warning highlights the presence of support and guidance for Musa (AS) from unexpected sources.

questions.q: How did Musa leave Egypt?
opts: In secret, without any supplies or proper preparation
With a large group of followers
On a horse provided by the believing man
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) left Egypt secretly to avoid Pharaoh's soldiers, as he was fleeing after killing an Egyptian. He departed without supplies or preparation, relying on Allah's guidance and protection during his escape.

questions.q: Where did Musa head when he fled Egypt?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) fled Egypt after unintentionally killing a man and headed toward Madyan (Midian) to escape Pharaoh’s punishment. In Madyan, he found refuge and later married the daughter of Prophet Shuayb (AS).

questions.q: How long did it take Musa to reach Madyan?
opts: He traveled for days without food, eating leaves
The text does not specify the duration
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) traveled for several days to reach Madyan, enduring hunger and eating leaves to survive. This journey highlights his patience and reliance on Allah during difficult times.

questions.q: What prayer did Musa make when he was heading toward Madyan?
opts: "My Lord, I am in need of whatever good You send down to me"
"My Lord, guide me to the right path"
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) was heading toward Madyan, he prayed to Allah expressing his complete reliance and need for any good that Allah would provide, showing his humility and trust in divine support during a time of uncertainty. This prayer reflects the importance of seeking Allah’s help in all matters.

questions.q: Where exactly in Madyan did Musa arrive?
opts: At a well where shepherds were watering their flocks
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) arrived at a well in Madyan where shepherds were watering their flocks, as this was a common gathering place and a key location for meeting the people of Madyan, including the family of Prophet Shuayb (AS). This setting marks the beginning of his mission there.

questions.q: Who did Musa see at the well?
opts: Two women holding back their flocks
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) saw two women at the well who were holding back their flocks because other shepherds were preventing them from watering their animals. This event marked the beginning of Musa’s journey in Egypt and demonstrated his kindness and willingness to help others.

questions.q: Why were the two women holding back their flocks?
opts: Because they were afraid of the other shepherds
Because they were waiting for the shepherds to finish so they could water their animals without mixing with men
Because their animals were not thirsty
questions.explanation: The two women held back their flocks to wait for the shepherds to finish watering, ensuring their animals drank without mixing with men, reflecting social customs of modesty and separation between genders at that time.

questions.q: What did Musa do when he saw this?
opts: He went and watered their flocks for them
He asked them why they were waiting
He chased away the other shepherds
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) saw the two women struggling to water their flocks, he kindly offered to help by watering their sheep for them, demonstrating his compassion and willingness to assist others in need.

questions.q: How did Musa water their flocks?
opts: He moved a heavy stone from the well that many men together could barely move
He asked the shepherds to move
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) watered the flocks by moving a heavy stone covering a well, which was so heavy that many men together could barely move it. This act demonstrated his strength and responsibility before prophethood.

questions.q: After watering their flocks, what did Musa do?
opts: He went and sat in the shade, praying
questions.explanation: After watering the flocks, Musa (AS) sought rest and spiritual connection by sitting in the shade and praying, demonstrating his devotion and reliance on Allah before continuing his journey.

questions.q: What was Musa's prayer while sitting in the shade?
opts: "My Lord, I am in need of whatever good You send down to me"
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) prayed, "My Lord, I am in need of whatever good You send down to me," expressing his complete reliance on Allah's mercy and provision while resting in the shade, showing humility and trust in God's support during his journey.

questions.q: What did the two women do after going home?
opts: They told their father about the man who helped them
questions.explanation: After the two women returned home, they informed their father about the man who helped them water their flock, highlighting his kindness and strength. This act led to Musa (AS) being invited to their home, marking the beginning of an important relationship in his life.

questions.q: What did the father say to one of his daughters when she told him about Musa?
questions.explanation: When one of the daughters of Prophet Musa's (AS) father told him about Musa, he responded by instructing her to invite Musa to their home, showing his willingness to welcome and support Musa after hearing about him. This reflects the importance of hospitality and trust in family.

questions.q: How did the daughter approach Musa?
questions.explanation: Musa's daughter approached him with shyness and modesty (tamshi `ala istihya') to show respect and humility, reflecting the cultural values of modest behavior when meeting someone new, especially in a serious or important situation. This behavior highlights her noble character and the respectful manner of their encounter.

questions.q: What did the daughter say to Musa?
opts: "My father invites you to reward you for watering our flocks"
questions.explanation: The daughter of the Midianite priest said this to Musa (AS) after he helped water their flocks, showing gratitude and inviting him to meet her father as a reward for his kindness and assistance. This highlights the importance of good character and helping others in Islam.

questions.q: Who was the father of the two women?
opts: A righteous man who was a descendant of Shu'ayb (most likely Shu'ayb himself according to Ibn Kathir)
questions.explanation: The father of the two women who helped Prophet Musa (AS) was a righteous man descended from Prophet Shu'ayb (AS), often identified as Shu'ayb himself by scholars like Ibn Kathir. This lineage highlights the piety and noble character of the family Musa (AS) encountered in Midian.

questions.q: When Musa met the father, what did the father say to reassure him?
opts: "You are safe here; do not fear"
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) met the father of the two disputing men, the father reassured him by saying, "You are safe here; do not fear," to comfort Musa and show trust, indicating that he was in a secure and peaceful environment despite the conflict.

questions.q: What did Musa tell the father about his situation?
opts: He told him everything about killing the Egyptian and fleeing
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) confessed to his father that he had killed an Egyptian who was oppressing a fellow Israelite and then fled Egypt to escape punishment. This honesty reflects his awareness of the consequences and his trust in his father's guidance.

questions.q: What did one of the daughters suggest to her father regarding Musa?
opts: "Hire him, for the best you can hire is the strong and trustworthy"
"Give him money and send him away"
"Kill him before Pharaoh's men find him"
questions.explanation: One of the daughters recognized Musa’s strength and trustworthiness when he helped them water their flock, suggesting to their father to hire him because qualities like strength and reliability are highly valued in a servant or worker.

questions.q: What evidence of Musa's strength did the daughter mention?
opts: He moved the heavy stone from the well alone
questions.explanation: Musa's strength was shown when he moved the heavy stone covering the well's opening by himself, allowing water access for the shepherds. This act demonstrated his physical power and kindness, which impressed the well owner's daughter and led to his employment.

questions.q: What evidence of Musa's trustworthiness did the daughter mention?
opts: He refused to look at her when she walked in front of him (he asked her to walk behind and give directions)
He was honest about his past
questions.explanation: Musa's refusal to look directly at the daughter demonstrated his modesty and respect, qualities that showed his trustworthiness and good character. This behavior impressed her and her family, indicating his strong moral values even before his prophethood.

questions.q: What proposal did the father make to Musa?
opts: To work for him for eight years in exchange for marrying one of his daughters
questions.explanation: Musa's father proposed that Musa work for him for eight years as a form of dowry, after which Musa would marry one of his daughters. This arrangement was a customary practice in that culture to establish marriage through service.

questions.q: What was the alternative term offered?
questions.explanation: Prophet Musa (AS) asked Pharaoh for an alternative term of ten years to allow his people to worship Allah freely and prepare for their departure, instead of immediate confrontation. This period was meant to give Pharaoh time to reconsider his oppression.

questions.q: What did the father say about these terms?
opts: "I intend to marry you to one of these daughters of mine on condition that you serve me for eight years; but if you complete ten, it will be of your own accord"
questions.explanation: The father of the daughters of Prophet Musa (AS) offered a marriage proposal conditional on serving him for eight years, with an option to extend to ten years voluntarily. This highlights the importance of fulfilling agreements and the value of patience and commitment in Islamic teachings.

questions.q: Which of the two terms did Musa fulfill according to authentic hadith?
opts: The longer and more complete term (10 years)
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) was commanded to stay in Midian for a specific period before returning to Egypt, and authentic hadith indicate he fulfilled the longer term of 10 years, demonstrating his patience and obedience to Allah’s command. This period allowed him to prepare for his prophetic mission.

questions.q: Which of the two daughters did Musa marry?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) married the younger daughter of Prophet Shuayb (AS) after helping her and her sister water their flock. This is mentioned in the Quran (Surah Al-Qasas 28:27), highlighting Musa's patience and kindness before his prophethood.

questions.q: What was the name of Musa's wife?
questions.explanation: Musa's wife is known as Safura (or Zipporah), the daughter of Prophet Shuayb (AS). She accompanied Musa (AS) when he fled Egypt and supported him during his mission to free the Israelites.

questions.q: How long did Musa stay in Madyan?
opts: He served his term and then some additional time
The text does not specify exactly, but he completed the term and then left with his family
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) stayed in Madyan until he completed the term of service he agreed upon to help the family of the two women, after which he left with his family. The exact duration is not specified in the Quran or authentic texts.

questions.q: What did Musa's father-in-law give him when he left?
opts: His staff (which became the staff of prophethood)
questions.explanation: Musa's father-in-law, Prophet Shuayb (AS), gave him his staff as a symbol of support and guidance. This staff later became a miraculous tool through which Musa (AS) performed signs and miracles, affirming his prophethood.

questions.q: Why did Musa decide to leave Madyan with his family?
opts: He felt a longing to return to Egypt
He completed his term and wanted to see his mother
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) left Madyan with his family because he felt a strong desire to return to Egypt, where he was born and where his mission to confront Pharaoh and guide his people awaited. This longing was both personal and tied to his prophetic responsibility.

questions.q: What happened during the journey back to Egypt?
opts: Musa saw a fire on the side of Mount Tur (Sinai)
questions.explanation: During the journey back to Egypt, Musa (AS) saw a fire on the side of Mount Tur (Sinai), which was a significant moment as it was where Allah spoke to him and appointed him as a prophet to guide the people of Egypt. This event marks the beginning of his prophetic mission.

questions.q: What did Musa say to his family when he saw the fire?
opts: "Stay here, I have seen a fire; perhaps I can bring you a torch or find some guidance at the fire"
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) saw the fire, he told his family to stay put because he hoped to bring back a torch for warmth or light, or possibly find guidance from the fire, indicating his cautious and hopeful approach to the unknown.

questions.q: When Musa approached the fire, what did he see?
opts: Fire in a bush that was not consuming it
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) approached the fire, he saw a burning bush that was not being consumed by the flames, which was a miraculous sign from Allah. This event marked the moment Allah spoke to him and appointed him as a prophet to guide the Children of Israel.

questions.q: What was the voice that called out to Musa?
questions.explanation: The voice that called out to Musa (AS) was the voice of Allah, speaking to him directly from the burning bush. This divine communication marked the beginning of Musa's prophethood and his mission to guide the Children of Israel.

questions.q: What was the first thing Allah said to Musa?
opts: "O Musa, I am Allah, the Lord of the worlds"
"O Musa, remove your shoes, for you are in the sacred valley of Tuwa"
"O Musa, I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed"
"O Musa, go to Pharaoh, for he has transgressed"
questions.explanation: When Allah first spoke to Musa (AS) at Mount Tuwa, He instructed him to remove his shoes as a sign of respect and purity, since the place was sacred. This emphasizes the importance of reverence and cleanliness in the presence of Allah.

questions.q: Why was Musa commanded to remove his shoes?
opts: As a sign of respect for the sacred valley
Because they were made of animal skin
Because he needed to feel the ground
questions.explanation: Musa was commanded to remove his shoes as a sign of respect and humility because he was standing on sacred ground, the holy valley of Mount Sinai, where Allah's presence was manifested. This act symbolizes reverence and purity in the presence of the divine.

questions.q: What did Allah tell Musa about Himself?
opts: "I am your Lord, so remove your shoes"
"Indeed, I am Allah. There is no god except Me, so worship Me and establish prayer for My remembrance"
