"I am the God of your fathers"
"I am the One who saved you from Pharaoh"
questions.explanation: Allah told Musa that He is the one true God, emphasizing His uniqueness and the importance of worshiping Him alone. He also instructed Musa to establish prayer as a way to remember and stay connected to Him.

questions.q: What was the first miracle given to Musa at the burning bush?
opts: The staff turning into a snake
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) was given two miracles at the burning bush: his staff turning into a snake and his hand becoming shining white. These signs were meant to prove his prophethood and help him confront Pharaoh.

questions.q: What happened when Musa threw his staff?
opts: It became a moving serpent (thu`banun mubin)
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) threw his staff, it transformed into a moving serpent as a sign from Allah to demonstrate his prophethood and to challenge the magicians of Pharaoh. This miracle showed Allah’s power and helped Musa convince Pharaoh to free the Israelites.

questions.q: How did Musa react to the serpent?
opts: He turned and fled, not looking back
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) saw the serpent, he was initially frightened and turned to flee without looking back, showing his human fear. This reaction highlights his natural response before Allah reassured him and strengthened his resolve to confront Pharaoh.

questions.q: What did Allah tell Musa to do with the serpent?
opts: Seize it and fear not; it would return to its original state
questions.explanation: Allah instructed Musa (AS) to seize the serpent without fear because it was a sign from Allah that would return to its original form, demonstrating Allah’s power and serving as a miracle to support Musa’s mission to Pharaoh.

questions.q: What was the second miracle?
opts: Putting his hand into his garment and bringing it out shining white, without harm
questions.explanation: When Prophet Musa (AS) put his hand into his garment and brought it out shining white without harm, it was a clear sign from Allah to prove his prophethood and to convince Pharaoh of his divine mission. This miracle demonstrated Allah’s power and helped establish Musa’s credibility.

questions.q: What was the purpose of this second miracle?
opts: To show it was a different kind of sign
To prove he was not a leper
questions.explanation: The second miracle given to Prophet Musa (AS) served to strengthen his message by showing clear signs of Allah’s power and to convince Pharaoh and his people to believe in the oneness of Allah and release the Israelites from bondage. This dual purpose emphasized both proof and persuasion.

questions.q: What request did Musa make to Allah at the burning bush?
opts: That his brother Harun be appointed as his helper
questions.explanation: When Allah spoke to Musa at the burning bush, Musa requested that his brother Harun be appointed as his helper to support him in conveying Allah’s message to Pharaoh, as Harun was more eloquent in speech and could assist in the mission effectively.

questions.q: Why did Musa ask for Harun?
opts: Because Harun was more eloquent and could speak better than Musa
Because Harun was already in Egypt
questions.explanation: Musa asked for Harun because he recognized that Harun had greater eloquence and communication skills, which would help effectively convey Allah’s message to Pharaoh and his people. This partnership ensured the message was delivered clearly and persuasively.

questions.q: Did Allah grant Musa's request?
opts: Yes, He appointed Harun as a prophet and Musa's helper
He told Musa to manage alone
questions.explanation: Allah granted Musa's request by appointing his brother Harun as a prophet and helper to support him in conveying the message to Pharaoh and the people of Egypt, ensuring Musa would not face the mission alone.

questions.q: What did Allah tell Musa about the future?
opts: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has transgressed"
questions.explanation: Allah instructed Musa (AS) to confront Pharaoh because he was unjust and oppressive, warning Musa to deliver Allah’s message and demand the release of the Israelites from slavery. This marked the beginning of Musa’s mission to challenge Pharaoh’s tyranny and lead his people to freedom.

questions.q: How did Musa respond to this command?
opts: He expressed fear of Pharaoh killing him as he had killed the Egyptian
questions.explanation: Musa expressed fear of Pharaoh killing him because he had previously killed an Egyptian man, which made him worried about Pharaoh’s reaction and possible punishment. This shows Musa’s awareness of the danger he faced in confronting Pharaoh.

questions.q: What reassurance did Allah give Musa?
opts: "I will be with you and Harun, hearing and seeing"
questions.explanation: Allah reassured Musa by promising His constant presence and support, emphasizing that He is always hearing and seeing, which gave Musa confidence and strength to face the challenges ahead with Harun.

questions.q: What did Allah tell them to say to Pharaoh?
opts: "We are the messengers of the Lord of the worlds, so send with us the Children of Israel"
questions.explanation: Allah instructed Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) to tell Pharaoh that they were His messengers and to demand the release of the Children of Israel from bondage, emphasizing their divine authority and mission to free their people.

questions.q: How did Musa and Harun approach Pharaoh's court?
opts: They waited at the gate for permission
They came with a large crowd
questions.explanation: Musa and Harun approached Pharaoh's court respectfully by waiting at the gate for permission, demonstrating patience and humility before entering. This showed their awareness of Pharaoh's authority and their reliance on Allah's guidance in delivering their message.

questions.q: What did Pharaoh say when they finally stood before him?
opts: "Did we not raise you among us as a child, and you remained among us for years of your life? And you did your deed which you did, and you are of the ungrateful"
questions.explanation: Pharaoh reminded Musa (AS) of his upbringing in the palace to question his loyalty, accusing him of ingratitude for opposing him despite being raised among the Egyptians. This highlights Pharaoh’s attempt to undermine Musa’s message by appealing to personal history rather than addressing the truth of his mission.

questions.q: How did Musa respond to Pharaoh's reminder of his upbringing?
opts: He acknowledged it but explained it was before prophethood
He said, "I did it then, and I was of those astray. So I fled from you when I feared you. Then my Lord granted me wisdom and made me one of the messengers"
questions.explanation: Musa acknowledged his past mistakes by admitting he was once misguided and explained that he fled Pharaoh out of fear. He emphasized that Allah later granted him wisdom and prophethood, showing his transformation and divine guidance after his earlier actions.

questions.q: What was the first sign Musa showed Pharaoh?
opts: The staff turning into a snake
He performed a miracle with water
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) threw down his staff, it miraculously transformed into a snake, demonstrating Allah's power and serving as a clear sign to Pharaoh to believe in Musa’s prophethood. This miracle was meant to challenge Pharaoh’s authority and the false gods of Egypt.

questions.q: How did Pharaoh react when he saw the staff become a serpent?
opts: He was terrified and fell off his throne
He accused Musa of being a magician
questions.explanation: Pharaoh reacted with disbelief and fear when Musa's staff turned into a serpent, accusing him of using magic to deceive the people. This was because such a miraculous sign challenged Pharaoh's authority and the beliefs of his court.

questions.q: What did Pharaoh do next to counter Musa's miracle?
opts: He challenged Musa to a duel
He summoned the best magicians in his kingdom
questions.explanation: Pharaoh summoned the best magicians in his kingdom to challenge Musa's miracle, as they were skilled in illusions and magic, aiming to discredit Musa's divine signs and maintain Pharaoh's authority over the people.

questions.q: How did Pharaoh gather the magicians?
opts: He sent messengers to all the cities
He only called those in his capital
questions.explanation: Pharaoh sent messengers to all the cities to gather the magicians because he wanted to challenge Prophet Musa’s (AS) miracles and prove his own power, demonstrating his control over the land and its people. This widespread summons ensured he collected the most skilled magicians available.

questions.q: What promise did Pharaoh make to the magicians?
opts: That they would be rewarded with gold
That they would be among his closest associates if they defeated Musa
questions.explanation: Pharaoh promised the magicians that if they could outmatch Musa (AS) in their contest of miracles, they would be honored as his closest advisors and receive a high status in his court, showing his reliance on their skills to maintain his power.

questions.q: How did the magicians feel about their chances?
opts: They were confident they could defeat Musa
They thought Musa was no match
questions.explanation: The magicians were confident they could defeat Musa because they relied on their practiced magic tricks, believing their skills were superior to his miracles. However, Musa's miracles, given by Allah, surpassed their illusions, demonstrating divine power over human abilities.

questions.q: What did the magicians ask Musa before the contest?
opts: "Either you throw first, or we will be the first to throw"
questions.explanation: The magicians asked Musa whether he would throw his staff first or they would, trying to control the sequence of the contest to gain an advantage. This shows their attempt to challenge Musa and test his confidence before the miracle of his staff turning into a serpent.

questions.q: What did Musa say to them?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) said "Throw what you will throw" to the magicians of Pharaoh when they challenged him, showing his confidence in Allah's power. This phrase signified his readiness to demonstrate the miracle given to him by Allah, which would surpass their magic.

questions.q: What did the magicians throw?
opts: Ropes and staffs that appeared to be snakes through their magic
questions.explanation: The magicians threw ropes and staffs that, through their magic, appeared to transform into snakes to challenge Prophet Musa (AS). This illusion was meant to mimic the miracle of Musa's staff turning into a snake by Allah's power.

questions.q: How did Musa feel when he saw their illusions?
opts: He felt a secret fear within himself
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) felt a secret fear when he saw the illusions because they challenged the clear signs from Allah, creating doubt and uncertainty in a difficult situation. This human emotion highlights his sincerity and reliance on Allah despite the deceptive tricks of Pharaoh’s magicians.

questions.q: What did Allah reveal to Musa at that moment?
opts: "Do not be afraid, for you are superior"
"Throw what is in your right hand"
questions.explanation: Allah revealed to Musa (AS) to "Throw what is in your right hand" as a sign to demonstrate His power, causing Musa's staff to transform into a serpent, which was a miracle to challenge Pharaoh's magicians and prove Allah's supremacy.

questions.q: How did the magicians react when they saw this?
opts: They fell down in prostration, declaring belief in the Lord of Musa and Harun
questions.explanation: When the magicians saw the miracles performed by Musa (AS) through Allah's power, they recognized the truth and submitted by prostrating, acknowledging the greatness of the Lord of Musa and Harun (AS), showing their acceptance of monotheism and Allah's supreme authority.

questions.q: Why did the magicians prostrate?
opts: They recognized that what Musa did was not magic but a divine miracle
questions.explanation: The magicians prostrated because they realized that Musa’s staff turning into a serpent was not ordinary magic, but a true miracle from Allah, demonstrating His supreme power beyond human trickery. This acknowledgment showed their faith in the divine message Musa brought.

questions.q: What did the magicians say when they prostrated?
opts: "We believe in the Lord of the worlds, the Lord of Musa and Harun"
questions.explanation: When the magicians witnessed the miracle of Musa’s staff turning into a serpent, they realized the truth of his message and declared their faith by saying, "We believe in the Lord of the worlds, the Lord of Musa and Harun," acknowledging the power and authority of Allah over all.

questions.q: How did Pharaoh react to the magicians' belief?
opts: He was furious and threatened them with severe punishment
questions.explanation: Pharaoh was furious because the magicians' belief in Musa's message challenged his authority and threatened his control over the people. He responded with anger and threatened severe punishment to intimidate them and maintain his power.

questions.q: What punishment did Pharaoh threaten the magicians with?
opts: Cutting off their hands and feet on opposite sides and crucifying them
questions.explanation: Pharaoh threatened the magicians with cutting off their hands and feet on opposite sides and crucifying them to intimidate and prevent them from supporting Prophet Musa (AS). This severe punishment aimed to maintain his authority and suppress any challenge to his power.

questions.q: How did the magicians respond to Pharaoh's threats?
opts: They said, "No harm! Indeed, to our Lord we will return. We hope that our Lord will forgive us our sins because we were the first of the believers"
questions.explanation: The magicians responded to Pharaoh's threats with faith and repentance, acknowledging their return to Allah and hoping for His forgiveness, showing their belief in Musa's message despite Pharaoh's intimidation. This demonstrates their sincere acceptance of monotheism after witnessing the truth.

questions.q: What does the magicians' response demonstrate?
opts: Their true and sincere faith, preferring Allah's punishment over Pharaoh's
questions.explanation: The magicians' response shows their sincere belief in Allah, as they chose to face Pharaoh's punishment rather than deny the truth of Musa's message. This highlights their recognition of Allah's power and their commitment to faith despite the risks.

questions.q: According to some narrations, what happened to the magicians after they believed?
opts: Pharaoh carried out his threat and killed them
questions.explanation: After the magicians recognized the truth of Prophet Musa’s (AS) message and believed in Allah, Pharaoh, angered by their defiance, carried out his threat and killed them to maintain his authority and suppress their faith. This highlights the trials faced by believers under oppressive rulers.

questions.q: After the magicians believed, what did Musa and Harun continue to do?
opts: They continued to call Pharaoh and his people to faith
questions.explanation: After the magicians believed in the truth of Musa's message, Musa and Harun continued to call Pharaoh and his people to faith, urging them to abandon falsehood and worship Allah alone, emphasizing the importance of monotheism and righteousness.

questions.q: What did Pharaoh and his people ask Musa to do when the plagues came?
opts: To pray to his Lord to remove the plague, promising to believe
questions.explanation: When the plagues struck, Pharaoh and his people realized the power of Musa's Lord and asked him to pray for relief, promising to believe in Allah if the punishment was lifted. This shows their initial recognition of divine authority despite their earlier rejection.

questions.q: What happened each time Musa prayed and the plague was removed?
opts: They broke their promise and continued in disbelief
questions.explanation: Each time Musa (AS) prayed and the plague was lifted, the people broke their promise to obey Allah and returned to disbelief, showing their lack of true faith and commitment despite witnessing clear signs.

questions.q: What was the first plague mentioned in the detailed narration?
questions.explanation: The first plague mentioned in the detailed narration of Prophet Musa (AS) is the flood (Tufan), which was sent as a punishment to Pharaoh and his people for their oppression and disbelief. This event demonstrates Allah’s power and serves as a warning to those who reject His messengers.

questions.q: What was the nature of the flood plague?
opts: Water overflowed the land, flooding everything, and then stagnated
questions.explanation: The flood plague involved an overwhelming overflow of water that submerged the land, destroying crops and homes, followed by stagnant water that caused further harm through disease and decay. This punishment demonstrated Allah’s power and served as a warning to Pharaoh and his people.

questions.q: What effect did the flood have on the Egyptians?
opts: They were severely distressed and hungry
questions.explanation: The flood caused widespread destruction in Egypt, damaging crops, homes, and infrastructure, leading to hardship for the people. It demonstrated the power of Allah and served as a sign to Pharaoh and the Egyptians to heed the message of Prophet Musa (AS).

questions.q: What was the second plague?
questions.explanation: The second plague sent upon Pharaoh and his people was locusts, which devoured crops and vegetation, causing severe damage to their food supply. This was a divine punishment meant to compel Pharaoh to release the Children of Israel from slavery.

questions.q: What did the locusts destroy?
opts: Trees, crops, and even the iron nails of doors
questions.explanation: The locusts sent as a punishment during Prophet Musa's time destroyed trees, crops, and even iron nails of doors, showing the severity of the plague and the power of Allah's signs to compel Pharaoh to release the Children of Israel.

questions.q: What was the third plague?
questions.explanation: The third plague sent upon Pharaoh and his people was lice (Qummal), which caused great discomfort and showed the power of Allah through Prophet Musa (AS). This plague demonstrated that even small creatures could be a sign of divine punishment.

questions.q: Where did the lice come from according to the narration?
opts: From a sand dune that Musa struck with his staff
From the bodies of the Egyptians
questions.explanation: According to the narration, the lice appeared from a sand dune that Prophet Musa (AS) struck with his staff, demonstrating Allah’s power through miraculous signs to Pharaoh and his people. This event showed how small creatures can be created from seemingly lifeless matter by divine command.

questions.q: What was the fourth plague?
questions.explanation: The fourth plague sent upon Pharaoh and his people was an infestation of frogs that covered the land, disrupting their daily life. This plague demonstrated Allah’s power and was a sign for Pharaoh to release the Children of Israel from slavery.

questions.q: What did the frogs do?
opts: They filled their houses, food, and vessels
questions.explanation: The frogs were one of the plagues sent by Allah to Pharaoh and his people, and they multiplied so greatly that they filled their houses, food, and vessels, causing great distress and demonstrating Allah's power.

questions.q: What was the fifth plague?
questions.explanation: The fifth plague was a severe infestation of wild animals or flies, not blood. The plague of blood was actually the first plague, where the Nile River turned to blood, causing water to become undrinkable and fish to die. This was a sign from Allah to Pharaoh to release the Children of Israel.

questions.q: What was the nature of the blood plague?
opts: All their water, from the Nile to wells and containers, turned to blood
Only the drinking water turned to blood
questions.explanation: The blood plague was a divine punishment where all water sources in Egypt, including the Nile River, wells, and containers, turned to blood, making the water undrinkable and causing great distress. This miracle demonstrated Allah’s power and was a sign to Pharaoh to release the Israelites.

questions.q: What was the miracle regarding the plagues that proved they were from Allah through Musa?
opts: They only affected the Egyptians and not the Children of Israel
questions.explanation: The plagues targeted only the Egyptians, sparing the Children of Israel, demonstrating that these events were divine signs sent through Musa (AS) to show Allah’s power and to distinguish His chosen people from the oppressors. This selective impact proved the miracles were from Allah, not natural occurrences.

questions.q: How did the Egyptians react to the plagues?
opts: They asked Musa to pray for removal, promised to believe, but broke their promise each time
questions.explanation: The Egyptians initially pleaded with Musa (AS) to pray for the removal of each plague and promised to believe in Allah, but after the plague was lifted, they repeatedly broke their promise and returned to disbelief and oppression. This cycle demonstrated their stubbornness and rejection of the message.

questions.q: What does Allah say about this in the Quran?
opts: "But when We removed the punishment from them until a term they were to reach, at once they broke their word"
questions.explanation: This verse highlights how the people were given respite from punishment by Allah until a specified time, but they failed to keep their promise and broke their covenant, demonstrating human weakness and the consequences of breaking Allah’s commands.

questions.q: How many plagues were there in total?
questions.explanation: There were nine major plagues sent upon Pharaoh and his people as signs from Allah through Prophet Musa (AS), with some scholars including the drowning of Pharaoh and his army as a tenth calamity, marking the final punishment and deliverance of the Israelites.

questions.q: What was the final plague before the Exodus?
opts: The death of the firstborn of Egypt
questions.explanation: The final plague before the Exodus was the death of the firstborn of Egypt, which was a severe punishment from Allah to Pharaoh and his people for refusing to free the Israelites. This event led Pharaoh to finally release the Israelites from slavery.

questions.q: What did Allah command the Israelites to do on the night of the final plague?
opts: To mark their doors with blood so the angel of death would pass over them
questions.explanation: Allah commanded the Israelites to mark their doorposts with the blood of a lamb so that the Angel of Death would pass over their homes, sparing their firstborns during the final plague. This act was a sign of obedience and protection, leading to their eventual liberation from Pharaoh’s oppression.

questions.q: At what time did the Children of Israel leave Egypt?
opts: At midnight, after the plague of the firstborn
questions.explanation: The Children of Israel left Egypt at midnight following the plague of the firstborn, as this was the moment when Pharaoh finally allowed them to depart. This timing is significant because it marks the fulfillment of Allah’s promise and the beginning of their liberation from slavery.

questions.q: What was the state of the Egyptians when the Israelites left?
opts: They were in great mourning over their dead firstborn
questions.explanation: When the Israelites left Egypt, the Egyptians were in great mourning because the Angel of Death had struck down all their firstborn sons, causing immense grief and loss throughout the land. This event marked a significant moment of sorrow and realization for the Egyptians.

questions.q: How did the Israelites prepare their bread for the journey?
opts: They carried the dough before it had risen (unleavened bread)
questions.explanation: The Israelites carried the dough before it had risen because they left Egypt in haste, without time to let the bread rise. This is why they ate unleavened bread during their journey, symbolizing their swift departure and reliance on Allah's guidance.

questions.q: What did they take with them from Egypt?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) took the coffin of Prophet Yusuf (AS) with them when they left Egypt, fulfilling Yusuf's (AS) request to have his body carried back to the land of his ancestors. This act honored Yusuf's (AS) wish and symbolized the connection between the prophets and their heritage.

questions.q: Why did they take the coffin of Yusuf?
opts: To bury him in the Holy Land as he had requested
To prevent the Egyptians from keeping it
To use it in their worship
questions.explanation: Yusuf (AS) requested that after his death, his body be taken back to the Holy Land for burial. This was to fulfill his wish of resting in the land of his ancestors, so Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) took his coffin to honor that request.

questions.q: How did Allah guide them during their journey?
opts: With a pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night
With Musa's staff pointing the direction
questions.explanation: Allah guided Prophet Musa (AS) and the Israelites during their journey by providing a pillar of cloud to lead them by day and a pillar of fire to guide them by night, ensuring they could travel safely and know the direction to follow.

questions.q: What was the function of the cloud by day?
opts: To guide them and protect them from the sun
To hide them from the Egyptians
questions.explanation: The cloud by day served as a source of shade to protect the Israelites from the harsh sun and as a guide to lead them on the correct path during their journey in the desert. This divine guidance ensured their safety and comfort throughout their travels.

questions.q: What was the function of the pillar of fire by night?
questions.explanation: The pillar of fire by night served as a divine guide for the Israelites during their journey in the desert, providing light, direction, and protection. It symbolized Allah’s presence and guidance, ensuring the people knew the path to follow even in darkness.

questions.q: Where did the Israelites eventually camp?
questions.explanation: The Israelites camped by the Red Sea after their escape from Egypt, where Prophet Musa (AS) led them. This location was significant because it was where Allah parted the sea, allowing them to cross safely and escape Pharaoh’s pursuing army.

questions.q: What happened when they were camped by the sea?
opts: They saw Pharaoh and his army approaching
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) and the Children of Israel were camped by the sea, they saw Pharaoh and his army approaching to capture them, leading to the miraculous parting of the sea by Allah, allowing them to escape safely. This event demonstrated Allah’s power and protection over His prophets and followers.

questions.q: How did the Israelites react when they saw Pharaoh's army approaching?
opts: They became terrified and said, "We are surely to be overtaken"
questions.explanation: When the Israelites saw Pharaoh's army approaching, they became terrified because they feared being captured or killed, expressing their despair by saying, "We are surely to be overtaken." This reaction shows their initial lack of trust in Allah's plan despite Musa's (AS) leadership.

questions.q: What did Musa say to reassure them?
opts: "No! Indeed, with me is my Lord; He will guide me"
questions.explanation: Musa ﷺ reassured his people by expressing his complete trust in Allah’s guidance and support, showing that reliance on Allah provides strength and certainty even in difficult situations. This highlights the importance of faith and trust in Allah during challenges.

questions.q: What command did Allah reveal to Musa at that moment?
questions.explanation: Allah commanded Musa (AS) to strike the sea with his staff so that it would part, allowing the Israelites to escape from Pharaoh's pursuing army by crossing safely on dry land. This miracle demonstrated Allah's power and support for Musa and his people.

questions.q: What happened when Musa struck the sea?
opts: The sea parted into twelve dry paths, one for each tribe
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) struck the sea with his staff, Allah caused the sea to part into twelve dry paths, allowing each of the twelve tribes of Bani Israel to cross safely. This miraculous event demonstrated Allah’s power and helped the Israelites escape from Pharaoh’s army.

questions.q: How were the paths described?
opts: Wide roads with mountains of water on either side
questions.explanation: The paths were described as wide roads with mountains of water on either side to illustrate the miraculous parting of the sea by Prophet Musa (AS), allowing the Israelites to escape Pharaoh’s army safely. This vivid imagery emphasizes the divine intervention and the enormity of the miracle.

questions.q: What did the Israelites see when they looked at the walls of water?
opts: They could see each other through the water
questions.explanation: When the Israelites crossed the sea, the walls of water on both sides were so clear that they could see each other through them, which was a miraculous sign of Allah’s power and protection during their escape from Pharaoh.

questions.q: How did Pharaoh and his army react when they saw the sea parted?
opts: Pharaoh, in his arrogance, thought it was for him and led his army in pursuit
questions.explanation: Pharaoh arrogantly believed the parted sea was a sign of his power and advantage, so he confidently led his army to chase Musa (AS) and the Israelites, ignoring the warning signs of impending divine punishment.

questions.q: What did Pharaoh say to his people to motivate them?
opts: "Look how the sea has split for me to catch up with my runaway slaves"
questions.explanation: Pharaoh claimed the sea had split for him to pursue the Israelites, portraying himself as powerful and in control. This was meant to intimidate his people and justify his pursuit, despite the miraculous nature of the event being beyond his control.

questions.q: Who appeared to lead Pharaoh and his army into the sea?
opts: Jibril in the form of a rider on a mare, whose horse neighed at Pharaoh's horses, leading them in
questions.explanation: Angel Jibril appeared as a rider on a mare to lead Pharaoh and his army into the sea, causing confusion and fear by neighing at Pharaoh’s horses. This divine intervention ensured the destruction of the oppressors while protecting Prophet Musa (AS) and the believers.

questions.q: What happened when all of Pharaoh's army was inside the sea?
opts: Allah commanded the sea to close over them
questions.explanation: When all of Pharaoh's army entered the sea pursuing Musa (AS) and the Israelites, Allah commanded the sea to return to its normal state, causing the waters to close over the army and drown them, saving the believers and demonstrating Allah’s power and protection.

questions.q: What did Jibril do to Pharaoh at the moment of drowning?
opts: He took mud from the sea and stuffed it into Pharaoh's mouth to prevent him from uttering a sincere repentance
questions.explanation: At the moment Pharaoh was drowning, Jibril stuffed mud into his mouth to prevent him from sincerely repenting, ensuring Pharaoh's punishment was just because he rejected faith until the end. This act highlights that repentance must be genuine and timely to be accepted by Allah.

questions.q: Was Pharaoh's repentance accepted?
opts: No, because he believed only when he saw the punishment (at the point of death)
He will be resurrected as a believer
questions.explanation: Pharaoh's repentance was not accepted because he only believed in Allah's power when he faced immediate punishment at death, showing insincere faith. True repentance requires genuine belief and turning to Allah before facing consequences.

questions.q: What happened to Pharaoh's body?
opts: It was preserved as a sign for later generations
It was eaten by sea creatures
questions.explanation: Pharaoh's body was preserved after his death as a miraculous sign from Allah, demonstrating His power and serving as a warning to future generations about the consequences of arrogance and disbelief. This preservation is mentioned in the Quran to remind people of the fate of those who oppose God's messengers.

questions.q: On what day did this event occur according to the hadith?
questions.explanation: The event occurred on the 10th of Muharram, known as Ashura, a significant day in Islamic history when Prophet Musa (AS) and the Israelites were saved from Pharaoh's tyranny. This day is commemorated for its spiritual importance and acts of worship.

questions.q: What did the Prophet ﷺ say when he saw the Jews fasting on Ashura?
opts: "We have more right to Musa than you," and he fasted and commanded others to fast
"Fast on this day but not like them"
questions.explanation: The Prophet ﷺ acknowledged the significance of Ashura, observed by the Jews in remembrance of Musa (AS) and the parting of the sea, stating Muslims have a stronger connection to Musa. He fasted on that day and encouraged fasting to honor this important event in Islamic history.

questions.q: What did the Children of Israel see after crossing the sea?
questions.explanation: After crossing the sea, the Children of Israel saw people worshipping idols, which highlighted the widespread polytheism and false beliefs among the nations around them, contrasting with their own monotheistic faith taught by Prophet Musa (AS).

questions.q: What did the Israelites ask Musa when they saw the idol worshippers?
opts: "Make for us a god just as they have gods"
questions.explanation: The Israelites asked Musa to create a god for them like the idol worshippers because they were influenced by the polytheistic practices around them and struggled with maintaining pure monotheism, seeking a tangible deity to worship instead of the unseen Allah.

questions.q: How did Musa respond to this request?
opts: He said, "Indeed, you are a people behaving ignorantly. What they are engaged in is to be destroyed, and worthless is what they do"
He explained that they could have idols
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) responded firmly to the people's request by condemning their ignorance and the wrongful actions they were involved in, emphasizing that such behavior is destructive and meaningless in the sight of Allah. This shows his role as a guide calling people away from wrongdoing.

questions.q: What did Allah provide for the Israelites in the wilderness?
questions.explanation: Allah provided the Israelites in the wilderness with various blessings, including manna (a type of food), quails for meat, and water from rocks. These miracles sustained them during their journey after escaping Egypt, demonstrating Allah’s mercy and power.

questions.q: What was Manna?
opts: A sweet substance that came down with the morning dew
questions.explanation: Manna was a miraculous food provided by Allah to the Israelites during their journey in the desert, described as a sweet, dew-like substance that appeared each morning to sustain them. It symbolized Allah’s mercy and provision in times of hardship.

questions.q: What were the quails (Salwa)?
questions.explanation: The quails (Salwa) were birds sent by Allah to provide food for the Children of Israel during their journey in the desert, as mentioned in the Quran. They appeared in large numbers to help sustain the people when they were hungry.

questions.q: How did they get water in the wilderness?
opts: Allah commanded Musa to strike a rock with his staff, and twelve springs gushed forth for the twelve tribes
questions.explanation: When the Israelites were in the wilderness and thirsty, Allah instructed Prophet Musa (AS) to strike a rock with his staff, causing twelve springs to flow. This miracle provided water for each of the twelve tribes, demonstrating Allah’s mercy and support for His people through Musa’s leadership.

questions.q: What did Allah command them regarding the Sabbath (Saturday)?
opts: To observe it as a day of worship and rest
questions.explanation: Allah commanded the people to observe the Sabbath as a sacred day dedicated to worship and rest, refraining from work to honor Him. This was a test of their obedience and devotion, emphasizing the importance of spiritual reflection and discipline.

questions.q: What happened to those who violated the Sabbath by fishing?
questions.explanation: Those who violated the Sabbath by fishing were turned into apes as a divine punishment, illustrating the importance of obeying God's commandments. This story emphasizes the consequences of deliberately disobeying sacred laws given to the people of Prophet Musa (AS).

questions.q: Why did Musa go to Mount Sinai for forty days?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) went to Mount Sinai for forty days to receive the Tablets, which contained the Torah, a divine scripture with guidance and laws for the Children of Israel. This period was essential for spiritual preparation and communication with Allah.

questions.q: Who did Musa leave in charge of the people while he was gone?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) appointed his brother Harun (AS) to lead the people during his absence because Harun was knowledgeable, trustworthy, and capable of guiding them in both religious and social matters. This ensured continuity in leadership and maintained order among the Israelites.

questions.q: What did Harun tell the people before Musa left?
opts: "You have been tried by this, so be patient"
questions.explanation: Harun advised the people to remain patient and steadfast during Musa’s absence, emphasizing that their situation was a test from Allah. This guidance encouraged them to trust in Allah’s plan and avoid falling into misguidance or chaos.

questions.q: Who was Al-Samiri?
opts: A man from the Samaritans who had hypocrisy in his heart
questions.explanation: Al-Samiri was a man from the Samaritan community who led the Israelites astray by creating the golden calf during Prophet Musa’s absence, showing hypocrisy and disbelief despite witnessing Allah’s miracles.

questions.q: What did Al-Samiri do while Musa was away?
opts: He led the people in prayer
He collected jewelry from the people and made a golden calf
questions.explanation: Al-Samiri gathered gold jewelry from the Israelites and melted it to create a golden calf, which they then worshipped, leading to idolatry during Musa's absence. This act was a major sin, as it violated the commandment to worship only Allah.

questions.q: Where did Al-Samiri get the idea to make a calf?
opts: He had seen the Egyptians worshipping cows
He saw Jibril on his horse and took dust from the hoofprint, which gave the calf the ability to make sounds
questions.explanation: Al-Samiri saw the angel Jibril riding a horse and took dust from the horse’s hoofprint, which he used to create the golden calf. This dust gave the calf the ability to produce sounds, misleading the people into worshipping it.

questions.q: What did Al-Samiri tell the people about the calf?
opts: "This is your god and the god of Musa"
questions.explanation: Al-Samiri misled the people by claiming the calf was their god and the god of Musa (AS), encouraging them to worship it instead of Allah. This was a falsehood intended to divert them from true monotheism during Musa’s absence.

questions.q: What sound did the calf make?
opts: It made a lowing sound (like a cow's moo)
questions.explanation: The calf made a lowing sound, similar to a cow's moo, which was used to deceive the people into believing it was a living deity. This sound was significant because it showed how the idol was falsely given life and worshipped.

questions.q: How did the wind contribute to the sound?
opts: The calf was hollow, and wind passing through it created the sound
The dust from Jibril's horse made it alive
questions.explanation: The dust stirred by Jibril's horse created a sound that resembled the wind, making it seem alive and audible to Prophet Musa (AS). This phenomenon helped convey the presence and message of Allah in a way that Musa (AS) could perceive.

questions.q: Who among the Israelites worshipped the calf?
opts: Most of them, except for a small group led by Harun
questions.explanation: When Prophet Musa (AS) went to Mount Sinai, many Israelites lost patience and worshipped the golden calf, abandoning monotheism. However, a small group, guided by Harun (AS), remained faithful and refused to worship the idol, demonstrating steadfastness in faith.

questions.q: What did Harun do when he saw them worshipping the calf?
opts: He tried to stop them, saying "O my people, you are being tested. Indeed, your Lord is the Most Merciful, so follow me and obey my command"
questions.explanation: Harun (AS) actively opposed the worship of the calf, urging his people to remain faithful to Allah and follow his guidance, emphasizing that their actions were a test from Allah, who is Most Merciful. This shows his commitment to monotheism and his role as a prophet guiding his people away from idolatry.

questions.q: How did the people respond to Harun?
opts: They said, "We will never cease being devoted to the calf until Musa returns to us"
questions.explanation: When Harun (AS) tried to stop the people from worshipping the calf, they refused and declared their devotion would continue until Musa (AS) returned, showing their stubbornness and attachment to idolatry despite Harun's efforts to guide them back to monotheism.

questions.q: What did Harun fear would happen if he left them?
opts: He told Musa later: "I feared that you would say, 'You have divided among the Children of Israel'"
questions.explanation: Harun feared that if he left the Children of Israel alone, Musa might accuse him of causing division among them, so he stayed to support Musa and maintain unity among the people. This shows Harun’s concern for preserving harmony during a critical time.

questions.q: When did Musa return from Mount Sinai?
opts: After being informed by Allah of what happened
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) returned from Mount Sinai after Allah informed him about the actions of the Israelites during his absence, including the worship of the golden calf. This guidance helped him address the situation appropriately upon his return.

questions.q: How did Musa react when he returned and saw them worshipping the calf?
opts: He became intensely angry, threw down the Tablets, and seized his brother by the head, pulling him
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) was deeply angered because the people abandoned monotheism and worshipped the calf idol, violating God's command. His reaction—breaking the Tablets and confronting Harun (AS)—showed his distress over their disobedience and his urgency to restore their faith.

questions.q: What did the Tablets contain?
opts: The Torah, with guidance, mercy, and detailed explanations of all things
questions.explanation: The Tablets given to Prophet Musa (AS) contained the Torah, which included divine guidance, mercy, and detailed laws to help the Children of Israel live righteously. These teachings provided clear instructions on faith, morality, and social conduct.

questions.q: What was Harun's explanation to Musa?
opts: "Son of my mother, indeed the people overpowered me and were about to kill me. So do not let the enemies rejoice over me"
questions.explanation: Harun explained to Musa that the people had become so hostile that they nearly killed him, and he urged Musa not to let their enemies celebrate his downfall. This shows Harun’s concern for Musa’s safety and the seriousness of the opposition they faced.

questions.q: Did Musa accept Harun's explanation?
opts: Yes, he prayed for forgiveness for himself and Harun
questions.explanation: Musa accepted Harun's explanation because Harun clarified his actions and intentions, showing honesty and responsibility. Musa then prayed for forgiveness for both himself and Harun, demonstrating his humility and trust in his brother.

questions.q: What did Musa do to the calf?
opts: He burned it and scattered its ashes into the sea
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) burned the golden calf that the Israelites had made in his absence to reject idolatry and restore monotheism. He then scattered its ashes into the sea to completely remove the symbol of false worship and emphasize the importance of worshiping Allah alone.

questions.q: What punishment did Al-Samiri receive?
opts: Musa told him, "Go away! Indeed, for you in life is to say, 'No contact.' And indeed, you have an appointment [in the Hereafter] you will not fail to keep"
questions.explanation: Al-Samiri was punished by being ostracized and told to avoid contact with others during his lifetime as a consequence of leading the Israelites astray by creating the golden calf. This punishment served as a reminder of his wrongdoing and a warning of the inevitable accountability in the Hereafter.

questions.q: What was the punishment of Al-Samiri in this life?
opts: He would be isolated, with people avoiding him, saying "Do not touch"
questions.explanation: Al-Samiri was punished by being isolated from the community, with people avoiding him and saying "Do not touch," as a consequence of leading the Israelites to worship the golden calf during Prophet Musa's absence. This punishment served as a social deterrent and a sign of his wrongdoing.

questions.q: What did Musa ask Allah for regarding those who worshipped the calf?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) asked Allah to forgive those who worshipped the calf because they had sinned out of weakness and confusion. This shows his compassion and hope for Allah’s mercy, emphasizing the importance of forgiveness even for serious mistakes.

questions.q: What was the repentance required for those who worshipped the calf?
opts: That they kill each other (those who worshipped and those who didn't)
questions.explanation: The repentance required was a test of true faith, where those who worshipped the calf were commanded to kill each other to demonstrate sincere remorse and reject idolatry, ensuring only the genuinely repentant remained among the believers.

questions.q: Who did Musa choose to accompany him to Mount Sinai for the second time?
opts: Seventy elders from among the best of the Israelites
questions.explanation: Musa chose seventy elders from among the best of the Israelites to accompany him to Mount Sinai for the second time to receive guidance and laws from Allah, ensuring leadership and support for the community during this important spiritual event.

questions.q: Why did Musa choose these seventy?
opts: To apologize to Allah for the calf incident and seek forgiveness for the people
To witness the giving of the Torah
questions.explanation: Musa chose the seventy elders to intercede and seek Allah’s forgiveness on behalf of the people after they worshipped the calf, showing his responsibility as a prophet to ask for mercy and atonement for their mistake. This act demonstrated repentance and hope for Allah’s pardon.

questions.q: What did the seventy elders hear when they were with Musa?
opts: The voice of Allah speaking to Musa
questions.explanation: The seventy elders heard the voice of Allah speaking to Musa, which was a unique and direct communication from Allah to His Prophet. This event demonstrated the special status of Musa and the divine guidance given to him and his followers.

questions.q: What did the elders say when they heard Allah speaking to Musa?
opts: "We will not believe you until we see Allah plainly"
questions.explanation: The elders expressed doubt and demanded a clear, visible sign of Allah to believe Musa's message, showing their hesitation and need for direct proof of his prophethood. This highlights the challenge prophets faced in convincing their people through faith rather than physical evidence.

questions.q: What happened immediately after this demand?
opts: They were struck by a thunderbolt (rajfa) and died
questions.explanation: When the people of Pharaoh rejected Prophet Musa's (AS) demand to worship Allah alone, they were punished by a sudden thunderbolt (rajfa) that struck and killed them, demonstrating Allah's power and warning others against disbelief and defiance.

questions.q: How did Musa react to their death?
opts: He prayed to Allah, saying, "My Lord, if You had willed, You could have destroyed them before, and me. Would You destroy us for what the foolish among us have done?"
questions.explanation: Musa ﷺ responded to the death of his people with a heartfelt prayer, showing his deep concern and humility before Allah. He acknowledged that only Allah has the power to punish but pleaded for mercy, emphasizing that the mistakes were made by a few, not the entire community.

questions.q: How were they revived?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) revived the dead by praying to Allah, who granted him the power to bring them back to life as a sign to Pharaoh and his people, demonstrating Allah's supreme authority and supporting Musa's mission.

questions.q: What does this incident teach about seeing Allah in this world?
opts: It is impossible; no one can see Allah in this life
It is possible for prophets only
It will happen on the Day of Judgment
questions.explanation: Seeing Allah in this world is impossible because Allah is beyond human perception and physical sight. The incident with Prophet Musa (AS) illustrates that while prophets receive divine communication, direct vision of Allah is reserved for the hereafter.

questions.q: What did Allah tell Musa about His mercy after this incident?
opts: "My mercy is only for the righteous"
"My mercy encompasses all things, and I will decree it for those who fear Me and give zakah and believe in My signs"
questions.explanation: Allah told Musa that His mercy is vast and includes everything, and He grants it specifically to those who are conscious of Him (fear Him), give zakah (charity), and believe in His signs, emphasizing the importance of faith, righteousness, and charity in receiving divine mercy.

questions.q: What did Allah mention in this context about the Ummah of Muhammad?
opts: That they would be the best of nations
That they would follow an unlettered prophet they would find described in the Torah and Gospel
That they would be few in number
questions.explanation: Allah mentioned that the Ummah of Muhammad ﷺ would follow a prophet who is unlettered (illiterate) and whose coming was foretold in earlier scriptures like the Torah and Gospel, highlighting the continuity and confirmation of prophethood in Islamic belief.

questions.q: What did Musa ask after hearing about this future Ummah?
opts: To be part of that Ummah
All of the above, with longing
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) expressed deep longing and hope upon hearing about the future Ummah, asking questions that reflected his desire to understand their qualities and fate. This shows his concern and connection to the people who would follow his teachings.

questions.q: How did the Tablets look when Musa brought them back?
opts: They were broken (from when he threw them in anger)
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) saw his people worshipping the calf, he became very angry and threw the Tablets down, breaking them. This act symbolized the breaking of the covenant due to their disobedience and served as a powerful lesson about faithfulness to Allah's commands.

questions.q: What did the broken Tablets contain?
opts: Guidance and mercy for those who fear their Lord
questions.explanation: The broken Tablets given to Prophet Musa (AS) contained Allah’s commandments, which provided guidance and mercy for those who fear and obey their Lord. These laws were meant to help people live righteously and maintain a just society.

questions.q: Why did Allah command the Children of Israel to slaughter a cow?
questions.explanation: Allah commanded the Children of Israel to slaughter a cow so that its parts could be used to miraculously reveal the identity of a murder victim's killer, demonstrating His power and guiding them to justice.

questions.q: What was the background of this story?
opts: A wealthy man was killed, and his heirs disputed about who killed him
A poor man was killed, and no one knew who did it
questions.explanation: The story highlights a dispute among the heirs of a wealthy man who was murdered, illustrating the importance of justice and truth in resolving conflicts. Prophet Musa (AS) was involved in addressing such matters to ensure fairness and uphold divine law.

questions.q: Who was suspected of the murder?
opts: The man's nephew (or cousins) who wanted his inheritance
questions.explanation: The man's nephew (or cousins) was suspected of the murder because they wanted to claim his inheritance, which is a common motive in such cases. This story highlights the importance of justice and careful investigation in Islam.

questions.q: Where was the body found?
opts: At the door of one of the suspects
questions.explanation: The body was found at the door of one of the suspects to reveal the truth and identify the guilty party, demonstrating how Allah guides justice through clear signs in the story of Prophet Musa (AS).

questions.q: What did the people do when they found the body?
opts: They began arguing and blaming each other
questions.explanation: When the people found the body, they started arguing and blaming each other because they wanted to avoid responsibility for the murder. This reaction shows how fear and guilt can lead to conflict and division within a community.

questions.q: Who did they go to for judgment?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) was known as a prophet and judge who resolved disputes among the Children of Israel. People came to him for judgment because Allah granted him wisdom and authority to judge fairly according to divine guidance.

questions.q: What did Musa do when they brought the case to him?
opts: He asked Allah for the solution
He told them to figure it out themselves
questions.explanation: When a difficult case was brought to Musa (AS), he sought guidance from Allah, showing his reliance on divine wisdom rather than his own judgment. This teaches the importance of turning to Allah for help in making just decisions.

questions.q: What was Allah's initial command?
opts: To find the killer through investigation
questions.explanation: Allah's initial command to the people in the story of Musa (AS) was to slaughter a cow, which was a test of their obedience and sincerity. This command is mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, emphasizing the importance of following divine instructions precisely.

questions.q: How did the people react to this command?
opts: They mocked Musa, saying, "Do you take us in ridicule?"
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) conveyed Allah’s command, the people reacted with mockery because they doubted his message and saw it as a joke, reflecting their stubbornness and disbelief despite clear signs from Allah. This highlights the challenges prophets faced in calling people to faith.

questions.q: What did Musa say in response to their mockery?
opts: "I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant"
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) responded to the mockery by seeking protection from Allah against ignorance, showing his patience and reliance on Allah rather than reacting with anger or insult. This teaches the importance of humility and turning to Allah when faced with disrespect.

questions.q: What happened when they kept asking for details about the cow?
opts: The command became more specific and difficult
questions.explanation: When the people kept asking for more details about the cow, Allah's command through Prophet Musa ﷺ became increasingly specific and challenging to emphasize their reluctance and to test their obedience. This taught the importance of trusting and following divine instructions without unnecessary questioning.

questions.q: What was the first question they asked about the cow?
questions.explanation: The first question asked about the cow was regarding its age to ensure it was mature enough for sacrifice. This detail was important to fulfill the specific conditions of the command given to the Israelites in the story of the cow in Surah Al-Baqarah.

questions.q: What was the cow's description regarding age?
opts: It should be a young cow
It should be neither old nor young, but between the two
questions.explanation: The cow described to the Israelites was to be of moderate age, neither too old nor too young, symbolizing purity and suitability for sacrifice. This specific age ensured the animal was healthy and valuable, fulfilling the divine command given to Prophet Musa (AS) for the ritual.

questions.q: What was the second question they asked?
questions.explanation: The second question they asked was about the color, as it was meant to test Prophet Musa's (AS) knowledge and the truth of his mission by identifying specific details that only a true prophet could know. This demonstrated divine knowledge granted to him by Allah.

questions.q: What color was the cow specified to be?
opts: Bright yellow, pleasing to the beholders
questions.explanation: The cow specified in the story of Prophet Musa (AS) was described as bright yellow and pleasing to the beholders to ensure it was distinctive and easily recognizable, fulfilling Allah's command precisely as a test of obedience and faith for the Israelites.

questions.q: What was the third and final question they asked?
opts: Its description in more detail, as cows appeared similar to them
questions.explanation: The third question sought a more detailed description of the cow because the initial descriptions made the cows seem very similar, causing confusion. This clarification ensured the people could identify the exact cow to sacrifice, as instructed by Prophet Musa (AS).

questions.q: What was the final, most specific description of the cow?
opts: It should be a wild cow
It should not be submissive to plowing the earth or watering crops, free from any blemish, with no different color in it
It should be a work animal
questions.explanation: The cow described to Musa (AS) had to be strong and healthy, not used for labor like plowing or watering, completely free of defects, and uniformly colored to fulfill the specific command from Allah, emphasizing purity and obedience in the sacrifice.

questions.q: Where did they finally find such a cow?
opts: With a righteous young man who was devoted to his father
questions.explanation: They found the cow with a righteous young man devoted to his father, highlighting the importance of piety and respect within the community. This emphasizes that blessings and solutions often come through individuals who uphold strong moral and familial values.

questions.q: How much did they have to pay for the cow?
opts: Its weight in gold, and then ten times that amount according to some narrations
questions.explanation: They were instructed to pay the cow's weight in gold as a form of compensation, emphasizing the cow's value. Some narrations mention paying ten times that amount to highlight the importance of fulfilling the command precisely and to discourage any reluctance or delay.

questions.q: Why was the young man willing to sell only at such a high price?
opts: He knew they were desperate and the cow was rare
He didn't want to sell it because of its value to him
questions.explanation: The young man valued the item deeply, so he set a high price to discourage selling it easily. This shows his attachment and the importance he placed on it, reflecting how personal value can influence one's willingness to part with something.

questions.q: What did Allah command them to do after slaughtering the cow?
opts: To strike the dead man with a part of it
To offer it as a sacrifice
questions.explanation: After slaughtering the cow as commanded, Allah instructed the people to strike the dead man with a part of the cow. This act caused the dead man to come back to life and reveal the identity of his murderer, demonstrating Allah’s power and justice.

questions.q: What happened when they struck the dead man with a part of the cow?
opts: Allah brought him back to life
The cow came back to life
questions.explanation: When the believers were doubtful, Prophet Musa (AS) was commanded by Allah to strike the dead man with a part of the cow, and by Allah’s power, the man was brought back to life to show the truth of the message and demonstrate Allah’s ability to give life after death.

questions.q: What did the revived man say?
questions.explanation: When Prophet Musa (AS) revived the dead man by Allah’s permission, the man identified his killer as his own nephew, which was a key moment demonstrating Allah’s power to bring the dead back to life and establish justice.

questions.q: What happened to the revived man after naming his killer?
opts: He lived for many more years
questions.explanation: After being revived by Prophet Musa (AS) to identify his killer, the man died again as a sign of Allah’s power over life and death, demonstrating that only Allah controls life and death, and to confirm the truth of Musa’s (AS) mission.

questions.q: What does this story demonstrate about Allah's power?
opts: That He can bring the dead back to life
questions.explanation: This story demonstrates Allah's supreme power over all creation, showing that no magic or human ability can match His might. It highlights His control over life and events, affirming that true power belongs only to Allah.

questions.q: What did the Quran say about this event?
opts: "Thus does Allah bring the dead to life and shows you His signs that you might reason"
questions.explanation: This verse highlights Allah's power to give life to the dead, demonstrating His ability to perform miracles. It serves as a sign for people to reflect and understand the truth of His message through reasoning and faith.

questions.q: Who was Qarun (Korah)?
opts: An Israelite from the people of Musa, but he became arrogant
questions.explanation: Qarun was a wealthy Israelite during Prophet Musa’s time who became arrogant and boastful about his riches, forgetting Allah’s blessings. His pride led to his downfall, as Allah caused the earth to swallow him and his wealth as a punishment for his arrogance.

questions.q: What was Qarun's relationship to Musa?
opts: He was his cousin (son of Musa's paternal uncle)
questions.explanation: Qarun was Musa's cousin because he was the son of Musa's paternal uncle. This family connection is mentioned in the Quran to highlight Qarun's arrogance despite his noble lineage and blessings.

questions.q: What was Qarun known for?
questions.explanation: Qarun was known for his immense wealth, which he acquired through arrogance and misuse of his blessings. Despite his riches, he was ungrateful to Allah, leading to his downfall as a lesson in humility and reliance on God.

questions.q: How is Qarun's wealth described in the Quran?
opts: His treasures were so vast that the keys to them would weigh down a group of strong men
questions.explanation: Qarun's wealth is described as extraordinarily immense in the Quran, emphasizing his arrogance and misuse of riches. The heavy keys symbolize the vastness of his treasures, highlighting the moral lesson against pride and greed.

questions.q: What did Qarun's people advise him?
opts: Not to exult, for Allah does not love the exultant, but to seek the abode of the Hereafter with what Allah had given him
questions.explanation: Qarun's people advised him not to be arrogant or boastful about his wealth because Allah dislikes pride, and instead to use his blessings to prepare for the Hereafter, emphasizing humility and gratitude over arrogance.

questions.q: How did Qarun respond to this advice?
opts: He said, "I was only given it because of knowledge I possess"
questions.explanation: Qarun responded arrogantly, claiming his wealth was due to his own knowledge, showing pride and ingratitude. This attitude led to his downfall, as he ignored the advice to be humble and charitable.

questions.q: What did Qarun do to Musa?
opts: He plotted against him and tried to defame him
questions.explanation: Qarun was a wealthy man from the people of Musa (AS) who became arrogant and plotted against Musa by spreading false accusations to undermine his leadership and message. His actions were driven by jealousy and pride, aiming to defame Musa and challenge his authority.

questions.q: How did Qarun try to defame Musa?
opts: By hiring a woman to falsely accuse Musa of immoral conduct
By challenging him to a duel
questions.explanation: Qarun attempted to defame Musa by hiring a woman to falsely accuse him of immoral behavior, aiming to damage his reputation and undermine his leadership among the people. This tactic was meant to turn the community against Musa unjustly.

questions.q: What was Qarun's punishment?
opts: The earth swallowed him and his home
He was struck by a plague
questions.explanation: Qarun was punished because of his arrogance and refusal to acknowledge Allah's blessings; as a result, the earth swallowed him and his wealth, demonstrating that pride and greed lead to destruction.

questions.q: What did Qarun say when he was being swallowed?
opts: He cried out but was not saved
questions.explanation: Qarun cried out for help when he was being swallowed by the earth, but because of his arrogance and refusal to repent, Allah did not save him. This shows that sincere repentance is necessary for forgiveness, and arrogance leads to destruction.

questions.q: How did the people react when they saw Qarun being swallowed?
opts: Those who had desired his position the day before said, "Oh, how Allah extends provision to whom He wills of His servants and restricts it! If not that Allah had conferred favor on us, He would have caused it to swallow us"
questions.explanation: When Qarun was swallowed by the earth, those who envied his wealth realized that Allah grants and withholds provision according to His wisdom, acknowledging that their own safety was due to Allah's favor, not their own merit. This shows the importance of humility and recognizing Allah's control over wealth and status.

questions.q: What lesson does the story of Qarun teach?
opts: Arrogance and ingratitude lead to destruction, and true success is in the Hereafter
questions.explanation: Qarun's story teaches that arrogance and ingratitude towards Allah's blessings result in downfall, emphasizing that worldly wealth is temporary while true success lies in humility and preparing for the Hereafter.

questions.q: Who was the believing man of Pharaoh's family?
opts: An Egyptian who secretly believed in Musa
An Israelite who worked in Pharaoh's court
questions.explanation: The believing man of Pharaoh's family was an Egyptian who secretly accepted the message of Musa (AS) despite Pharaoh's opposition. He helped and supported Musa (AS) discreetly, showing faith in Allah while living under Pharaoh's rule.

questions.q: What did this man do when Pharaoh plotted to kill Musa?
opts: He came running from the farthest part of the city to warn Musa
He told Pharaoh not to do it
questions.explanation: When Pharaoh plotted to kill Musa (AS), a man quickly ran from the farthest part of the city to warn him, showing loyalty and concern for Musa’s safety. This act helped Musa avoid the plot and continue his mission.

questions.q: What did he say to Musa?
opts: "O Musa, the chiefs are conspiring against you to kill you, so leave. Indeed, I am to you of the sincere advisors"
questions.explanation: The statement was said by Harun (AS) to Musa (AS) to warn him about the plot by the chiefs against his life. This shows Harun’s loyalty and sincere advice to protect Musa from harm and ensure his safety.

questions.q: What did this believer say to his people to defend Musa?
opts: "Do you kill a man because he says 'My Lord is Allah'?"
questions.explanation: This believer defended Musa (AS) by questioning the injustice of killing someone simply for proclaiming faith in Allah, emphasizing the importance of justice and freedom of belief in Islam. This statement highlights the courage to stand for truth despite opposition.

questions.q: What arguments did he give to his people to prevent them from killing Musa?
opts: That Musa came with clear proofs from their Lord
That if Musa was lying, his sin would be on him, but if he was truthful, some of what he promised would befall them
That they should remember how Allah destroyed previous nations like 'Ad and Thamud
questions.explanation: Musa’s people were reminded of his noble character, his role as a messenger from Allah, and the miracles he showed as proof of his truthfulness. These arguments aimed to prevent them from harming him by emphasizing his divine mission and trustworthy nature.

questions.q: How did Pharaoh respond to this man's counsel?
opts: He said, "I only show you what I see, and I only guide you to the path of right conduct"
questions.explanation: Pharaoh responded to the counsel by asserting that he only shared his own perspective and aimed to guide people toward what he believed was right, reflecting his arrogance and refusal to accept the true message brought by Prophet Musa (AS). This highlights his stubbornness and rejection of divine guidance.

questions.q: What did the believing man say about the fate of the disbelievers?
opts: They will be punished in this world and the next
questions.explanation: The believing man explained that disbelievers face consequences both in this life and the Hereafter, emphasizing that rejection of faith leads to punishment in both worlds as a form of divine justice and warning.

questions.q: What was the final outcome for this believing man according to some narrations?
opts: He remained in Egypt and was saved from punishment
questions.explanation: According to some narrations, the believing man who supported Prophet Musa (AS) was ultimately killed by Pharaoh because of his faith and opposition to Pharaoh's tyranny, demonstrating the severe persecution faced by early believers.

questions.q: What did Allah command the Children of Israel to do after leaving Egypt?
opts: To enter the Holy Land (Bayt al-Maqdis) which He had decreed for them
questions.explanation: After leaving Egypt, Allah commanded the Children of Israel to enter the Holy Land (Bayt al-Maqdis) as it was the land He had promised and decreed for them to settle in and live according to His guidance. This was a fulfillment of His covenant with them.

questions.q: What did Musa do before entering the land?
opts: He sent scouts to gather information about the land and its people
questions.explanation: Before entering the land, Musa (AS) sent scouts to gather information about the terrain and the strength of its inhabitants to prepare his people for the challenges ahead, ensuring they were aware of what to expect and how to proceed wisely.

questions.q: How many scouts did Musa send?
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) sent twelve scouts, one from each of the twelve tribes of Bani Israel, to explore the land of Canaan. This was to gather information about its people and conditions before the Israelites entered it.

questions.q: What did the scouts report when they returned?
opts: The people were giants (Jabbarin), extremely tall and strong, and the scouts felt like insects in comparison
The land was fertile and ready for them
questions.explanation: The scouts reported that the inhabitants of the land were giants (Jabbarin), describing them as extremely tall and strong, which made the scouts feel insignificant and weak like insects. This report reflected their fear and lack of trust in Allah’s promise of victory.

questions.q: How did the majority of the Children of Israel react to this report?
opts: They refused to enter, saying, "O Musa, indeed we will never enter it as long as they are in it. So go, you and your Lord, and fight. Indeed, we are remaining right here"
questions.explanation: The majority of the Children of Israel reacted with fear and refusal to enter the promised land because they saw the inhabitants as too strong, showing a lack of trust in Allah’s promise and Musa’s leadership. This highlights their hesitation and reliance on human perception over divine assurance.

questions.q: Which two scouts encouraged the people to enter and fight?
opts: Yusha' ibn Nun and Caleb (Kalb) ibn Yufanna
questions.explanation: Yusha' ibn Nun and Caleb (Kalb) ibn Yufanna were the two scouts who trusted Allah's promise and encouraged the Israelites to enter the land and fight, showing faith and courage despite the challenges. Their positive report contrasted with the other scouts' fearful accounts.

questions.q: What did these two men say to the people?
opts: "Enter upon them through the gate, for when you enter it, you will be predominant. And upon Allah rely, if you should be believers"
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) instructed their people to enter the city through the gate, promising victory and dominance if they did so with trust in Allah. This emphasizes reliance on Allah combined with taking the correct action to achieve success.

questions.q: What was Allah's punishment for their refusal?
opts: They were forbidden from entering the land for forty years, wandering lost in the wilderness (at-Tih)
They were sent back to Egypt
questions.explanation: Because the Israelites disobeyed and refused to trust Allah's command to enter the promised land, they were punished by wandering in the wilderness (at-Tih) for forty years, during which they were barred from entering the land as a consequence of their lack of faith and obedience.

questions.q: What did Allah say about this punishment?
opts: "It shall be forbidden to them for forty years; they will wander throughout the land"
questions.explanation: Allah stated that due to their disobedience, the Israelites were forbidden from entering the Holy Land for forty years, during which time they would wander in the wilderness. This punishment served as a consequence for their lack of faith and trust in Him.

questions.q: Who was allowed to enter the land after the forty years?
opts: A new generation that grew up in the wilderness, led by Yusha' ibn Nun
questions.explanation: The original generation of Israelites was not allowed to enter the Promised Land due to their disobedience, so after forty years, a new generation that had grown up in the wilderness, led by Yusha' ibn Nun, was permitted to enter and inherit the land.

questions.q: What happened to Musa and Harun during this period?
opts: They died in the wilderness before entering
They led the people into the land
They were taken up to heaven
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) led the Israelites through the wilderness but were not allowed by Allah to enter the Promised Land due to certain disobediences. They passed away in the wilderness before reaching it, highlighting the importance of obedience and trust in Allah’s commands.

questions.q: What does the text mention about the battle of Yusha' against the giants?
opts: Musa raised his staff, and whenever he raised it, Yusha' prevailed; when his hand tired, the enemy prevailed
questions.explanation: During the battle of Yusha' against the giants, Musa (AS) used his staff as a sign of divine support; whenever he raised it, Yusha' gained the upper hand, but when his hand grew tired and he lowered it, the enemy would gain strength, showing reliance on Allah's help through Musa's leadership.

questions.q: Who helped Musa hold up his staff during the battle?
opts: Harun and another man named Hur
questions.explanation: During the battle against Pharaoh’s forces, Musa (AS) raised his staff as a sign of Allah’s power, but his arms grew tired, so his brother Harun (AS) and a man named Hur helped support his arms to keep the staff raised until victory was achieved.

questions.q: How long did the battle last?
questions.explanation: The battle lasted until sunset because, according to the story of Musa (AS), the fighting continued throughout the day and ended as the sun set, marking the conclusion of the conflict and allowing the people to rest. This timing emphasizes the intensity and duration of the struggle.

questions.q: What was the outcome of the battle?
questions.explanation: Yusha ibn Nun led the Israelites in the battle after Prophet Musa (AS) appointed him as their leader. With faith and determination, Yusha's party prevailed, demonstrating the importance of trust in Allah and strong leadership in overcoming challenges.

questions.q: Who was Hur mentioned in this context?
opts: A righteous man who accompanied them
The text identifies him as the husband of Musa's sister or a close associate
questions.explanation: Hur was either the husband of Musa's sister or a trusted companion who supported Musa during the confrontation with Pharaoh, helping to lead the Israelites when Musa was on Mount Sinai. His role highlights the importance of loyal support in difficult times.

questions.q: What did Musa find written in the Tablets about a future Ummah?
opts: They would be the best nation brought forth for mankind, enjoining good and forbidding evil
questions.explanation: The Tablets revealed that the future Ummah would be distinguished as the best nation, emphasizing their role in promoting good deeds and preventing evil. This highlights the responsibility given to the followers of Musa (AS) to uphold justice and moral values for the benefit of all humanity.

questions.q: When Musa asked Allah to make this Ummah his, what did Allah say?
questions.explanation: When Musa (AS) asked Allah to make the Ummah his own, Allah responded that the Ummah belongs to Prophet Ahmad (SAW), referring to the final Prophet Muhammad (SAW), indicating that the nation is ultimately connected to him. This highlights the special status of the Prophet Muhammad’s Ummah.

questions.q: What was unique about the scriptures of this Ummah?
opts: They would be written on gold
They would be in their hearts (they would memorize them)
They would be revealed all at once
questions.explanation: Unlike previous scriptures written on physical materials, the Quran was revealed to be memorized and preserved in the hearts of the believers, ensuring its protection and transmission through generations without alteration. This unique oral preservation is a distinctive feature of this Ummah's scripture.

questions.q: What did Musa learn about the charity (sadaqah) of this Ummah?
opts: It would be burnt if accepted
It would be eaten (consumed) by the poor, and they would still be rewarded
It would be given only to priests
It would be returned if not accepted
questions.explanation: Musa (AS) learned that the charity (sadaqah) given by this Ummah would be consumed by the poor, meaning it would be used to meet their needs, and despite this consumption, the givers would still receive reward from Allah for their sincere intention and generosity.

questions.q: How did previous nations deal with charity according to the text?
opts: If accepted, fire would come from heaven and consume it; if rejected, it would be left for wild animals
questions.explanation: Previous nations showed their attitude toward charity by either accepting or rejecting it; if they accepted, a divine fire would consume the offering as a sign of acceptance, but if they rejected it, the charity was left to be eaten by wild animals, indicating disapproval and divine rejection.

questions.q: What did Musa learn about the recording of deeds for this Ummah?
opts: When one intends to do a good deed but does not do it, it is written as one good deed
Deeds are only recorded if done publicly
